source.title: Traffic Classification, Encryption, and Observability Guidelines for Runtime Services
source.author: Priya Kapoor
source.body:
Overview
========

This document defines a cross-cutting standard for: (1) classifying runtime request traffic, (2) applying encryption and key-management requirements by deployment type, and (3) setting concrete observability (metrics, traces, logs) behaviors per traffic class. The goal is to ensure consistent operational posture across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private runtimes while enabling cost-aware telemetry and secure customer isolation.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all runtime services in the Redwood inference stack: api-gateway, request-router, model-runtime, kv-cache-proxy, autoscaler, and control-plane services.
- Excludes offline batch training jobs and external partner networks (those follow partner contracts).

Goals
-----
- Provide a minimal traffic taxonomy that guides routing, SLOs, and sampling.
- Mandate encryption and KMS practices appropriate to deployment sensitivity.
- Standardize telemetry shape, retention, and sampling so downstream analytics and alerting are reliable and cost-predictable.

Definitions and Traffic Taxonomy
-------------------------------
1. HOTPATH - Low-latency, user-facing inference requests with strict p95/p99 latency SLOs (e.g., hosted text generation).
2. COLDPATH - Non-interactive model operations (e.g., embeddings indexing, async reranking) where throughput matters more than single-request latency.
3. CONTROL - Control-plane/configuration and administrative requests (e.g., model updates, rollout commands).
4. BACKGROUND - Periodic maintenance, pre-warming, and housekeeping tasks.
5. BATCH - Large, scheduled inference jobs with explicit capacity reservations (Associated with Dedicated and Private).

Traffic Class Labels and Headers
-------------------------------
Services MUST propagate the following headers when a request is classified. These headers are used by telemetry, routing, and RBAC checks: 
- X-Redwood-Traffic-Class: HOTPATH | COLDPATH | CONTROL | BACKGROUND | BATCH
- X-Redwood-Tenant-ID: <tenant-id> (when applicable)
- X-Redwood-Request-Intent: sync | async | prewarm

Example HTTP snippet:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 
     -H "X-Redwood-Traffic-Class: HOTPATH" \ 
     -H "X-Redwood-Tenant-ID: tenant-42" \ 
     https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate

Observability Requirements (per traffic class)
--------------------------------------------
General notes: all metrics must include: service, route, model_id, traffic_class, tenant_id (when present). Traces must propagate traceparent and sampled flags according to sampling policy below. Logs must include request_id and enriched context for at least ERROR level events.

Metrics
- HOTPATH: p50/p90/p95/p99 latency for request and token generation, tokens/sec, concurrency, error-rate (4xx/5xx).
  - SLO example (HOSTED): p95 end-to-end latency < 150ms, availability 99.95% monthly.
- COLDPATH: throughput (items/sec), job-latency percentiles, queue depth.
- CONTROL: operation-duration, success-rate, and authorization-failures. Target success-rate >= 99.9% for critical control ops.
- BATCH & BACKGROUND: job completion time, retry-count, cost-estimate.

Tracing and Sampling
- HOTPATH: adaptive sampling with baseline 5% global + 100% for traces containing errors or latency > p95 for that route. Capture full span stack for sampled traces.
- COLDPATH/BATCH: 1% sampling by default; increase to 5% during anomalies.
- CONTROL: 100% sampling for rollout/change events; record config snapshot reference.

Logs
- Minimum log level for production services: INFO.
- ERROR and WARNING logs must be forwarded to the central log pipeline with tenant_id and request_id.
- For Private deployments with compliance requirements, set log retention and masking per customer contract (see "Data Retention & Masking" table).

Telemetry Tagging and Cost Control
- Each metric submission MUST include a cost_center tag (service-owned). The metrics ingestion pipeline will bill back based on cost_center and traffic_class to compute per-tenant telemetry costs.
- To avoid unbounded metric cardinality, cardinality limits per metric name: 50 values for model_id, 200 values for route, 1000 values for tenant_id. Alert if any metric exceeds cardinality limits for > 24 hours.

Encryption and Key Management
----------------------------
In-transit
- All network traffic between external clients and api-gateway MUST use TLS 1.3.
- Internal RPCs in Hosted and Dedicated networks MUST use mTLS with X.509 issued by Redwood PKI.
- Private deployments may opt to configure customer-managed CA; document required certificate CN format: CN=redwood-internal-<service>-<cluster>.

At-rest
- Model artifacts and KV caches on disk must be encrypted with AES-256.
- Snapshot storage (S3-compatible) must store objects with server-side encryption (SSE) enabled.

Key Management
- Hosted: Redwood-managed KMS (AWS KMS / GCP KMS) with keys rotated annually by default.
- Dedicated: Option to use Redwood-managed KMS or customer-provided keys.
- Private: Must support customer-provided KMS/HSM; require explicit configuration and verification during onboarding.
- All deployments: all key usage must be auditable; log key-access events to the audit-log service (control-plane-only) with retention per contract.

Rekeying and Compromise Plan
- Rekey cadence: rotate symmetric data keys every 12 months; master key rotation can be 36 months but on-demand rotation supported.
- Compromise procedure: revoke affected key, trigger transparent re-encryption job with bounded concurrency, notify security and affected tenants within SLA (24h for sensitive leaks). See Incident Response Security runbook for step-by-step actions.

Data Retention & Masking (summary table)
- Note: retention can be overridden by legal/compliance for specific customers.

| Data Type | Default Retention | Raw/Derived | Masking/Notes |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| Raw request logs (error-level) | 365 days (Private) / 90 days (Hosted) | Raw | Must be redacted for PII prior to indexing |
| Aggregated metrics (1m) | 5 years (aggregated) | Derived | High-resolution raw (15s) kept 7 days |
| Traces | 30 days (prod) | Raw | Store full payload only for sampled traces |
| Model artifacts | Indefinite (versioned) | Raw | Access controlled by KMS |

Routing and Fallback Behavior by Class
-------------------------------------
- HOTPATH: prefer lowest-latency models in catalog; fallback chain must be declared in model policy metadata (primary -> warmed-quantized -> small-variant). Fallback must preserve semantics and be transparent in logs.
- COLDPATH/BATCH: queue and batch aggressively; expose estimated start-time to caller header X-Redwood-Estimate-Start.
- CONTROL: must not be routed through tenant noisy-hosted clusters. Control ops are isolated to control-plane nodes.

Rollout and Change Management
----------------------------
All changes that affect traffic classification, telemetry shape, encryption, or header contracts MUST follow this rollout: 
1. Design review (-2 weeks): architectural review board signoff; include performance model and cardinality impact analysis.
2. Dev/Staging: apply new headers and telemetry to staging clusters; run synthetic load test with noise injection.
3. Canary: 5% traffic for 48 hours. Verify metrics: no >10% increase in p95 or >2x baseline error-rate.
4. Ramp: 25% for 72 hours, 50% for 48 hours, then 100% if stable.
5. Post-rollout: 30-day observation with weekly summary and rollback window of 14 days.

Operational Playbook (short)
---------------------------
- If cardinality alert fires: enable short-term metric aggregation and engage runtime-observability engineer. Steps:
  1. Identify offending label values via metrics-explorer.
  2. Apply tag suppression on router if noise is client generated.
  3. Rollback configuration change if introduced by the last deployment.

- If encryption audit fails (e.g., expired cert/missing key access): switch to backup key, notify security, open P1 incident.

Ownership and Contacts
----------------------
- Runtime Observability Team (primary): runtime-observability@redwood.ai. Owners: Marcus Li (lead), Ana Gómez.
- Infra-SRE (secondary): infra-sre@redwood.ai. Owner: Ethan Park.
- Security (review/incident): security@redwood.ai. Owner: Daniel Wu.

Examples and Snippets
---------------------
Telemetry emitter example (pseudo-YAML) for HOTPATH:

metrics:
  - name: request_latency_ms
    labels: [service, route, model_id, traffic_class, tenant_id]
    buckets: [1,5,10,25,50,100,250,500,1000]

Router policy example (JSON)
{
  "route": "/v1/generate",
  "traffic_class": "HOTPATH",
  "fallback_chain": ["gptx-13b", "gptx-6b-quant", "gptx-3b-small"],
  "telemetry_tags": {"cost_center":"inference-hosted"}
}

Migration Checklist for Existing Services
---------------------------------------
- Add X-Redwood-Traffic-Class header at service ingress.
- Emit tenant_id and traffic_class in metrics and traces.
- Verify mTLS between runtime components in staging.
- Run cardinality scanner and fix high-cardinality labels before canary.

Open Questions and Exceptions
----------------------------
- Special-case for low-throughput, privacy-sensitive tenants in Hosted: allow per-tenant retention overrides but require documented approval from Security and Legal.
- Legacy SDKs that cannot set headers: router will attempt to classify by route heuristics; teams must document heuristics and migrate SDKs within 6 months.

Related Documents
-----------------
- Telemetry ingestion and retention guidelines (see related_pages).
- ADR: Model slicing and fallback routing (see related_pages).

Revision History
----------------
- 2026-01-10: Created by Priya Kapoor (initial draft).
- 2026-02-18: Published after reviews by Marcus Li, Ana Gómez, and Security (updates to sampling and retention tables).

Approval
--------
Approved by: Architecture Review Board (signature stored in review artifacts).

- Runtime Observability Team (primary): runtime-observability@redwood.ai. Owners: Marcus Li (lead), Ana Gómez.
- Infra-SRE (secondary): infra-sre@redwood.ai. Owner: Ethan Park.
- Security (review/incident): security@redwood.ai. Owner: Daniel Wu.

Examples and Snippets
---------------------
Telemetry emitter example (pseudo-YAML) for HOTPATH:

metrics:
  - name: request_latency_ms
    labels: [service, route, model_id, traffic_class, tenant_id]
    buckets: [1,5,10,25,50,100,250,500,1000]

Router policy example (JSON)
{
  "route": "/v1/generate",
  "traffic_class": "HOTPATH",
  "fallback_chain": ["gptx-13b", "gptx-6b-quant", "gptx-3b-small"],
  "telemetry_tags": {"cost_center":"inference-hosted"}
}

Migration Checklist for Existing Services
---------------------------------------
- Add X-Redwood-Traffic-Class header at service ingress.
- Emit tenant_id and traffic_class in metrics and traces.
- Verify mTLS between runtime components in staging.
- Run cardinality scanner and fix high-cardinality labels before canary.

Open Questions and Exceptions
----------------------------
- Special-case for low-throughput, privacy-sensitive tenants in Hosted: allow per-tenant retention overrides but require documented approval from Security and Legal.
- Legacy SDKs that cannot set headers: router will attempt to classify by route heuristics; teams must document heuristics and migrate SDKs within 6 months.

Related Documents
-----------------
- Telemetry ingestion and retention guidelines (see related_pages).
- ADR: Model slicing and fallback routing (see related_pages).

Revision History
----------------
- 2026-01-10: Created by Priya Kapoor (initial draft).
- 2026-02-18: Published after reviews by Marcus Li, Ana Gómez, and Security (updates to sampling and retention tables).

Approval
--------
Approved by: Architecture Review Board (signature stored in review artifacts).
text:
Overview
========

This document defines a cross-cutting standard for: (1) classifying runtime request traffic, (2) applying encryption and key-management requirements by deployment type, and (3) setting concrete observability (metrics, traces, logs) behaviors per traffic class. The goal is to ensure consistent operational posture across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private runtimes while enabling cost-aware telemetry and secure customer isolation.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all runtime services in the Redwood inference stack: api-gateway, request-router, model-runtime, kv-cache-proxy, autoscaler, and control-plane services.
- Excludes offline batch training jobs and external partner networks (those follow partner contracts).

Goals
-----
- Provide a minimal traffic taxonomy that guides routing, SLOs, and sampling.
- Mandate encryption and KMS practices appropriate to deployment sensitivity.
- Standardize telemetry shape, retention, and sampling so downstream analytics and alerting are reliable and cost-predictable.

Definitions and Traffic Taxonomy
-------------------------------
1. HOTPATH - Low-latency, user-facing inference requests with strict p95/p99 latency SLOs (e.g., hosted text generation).
2. COLDPATH - Non-interactive model operations (e.g., embeddings indexing, async reranking) where throughput matters more than single-request latency.
3. CONTROL - Control-plane/configuration and administrative requests (e.g., model updates, rollout commands).
4. BACKGROUND - Periodic maintenance, pre-warming, and housekeeping tasks.
5. BATCH - Large, scheduled inference jobs with explicit capacity reservations (Associated with Dedicated and Private).

Traffic Class Labels and Headers
-------------------------------
Services MUST propagate the following headers when a request is classified. These headers are used by telemetry, routing, and RBAC checks: 
- X-Redwood-Traffic-Class: HOTPATH | COLDPATH | CONTROL | BACKGROUND | BATCH
- X-Redwood-Tenant-ID: <tenant-id> (when applicable)
- X-Redwood-Request-Intent: sync | async | prewarm

Example HTTP snippet:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 
     -H "X-Redwood-Traffic-Class: HOTPATH" \ 
     -H "X-Redwood-Tenant-ID: tenant-42" \ 
     https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate

Observability Requirements (per traffic class)
--------------------------------------------
General notes: all metrics must include: service, route, model_id, traffic_class, tenant_id (when present). Traces must propagate traceparent and sampled flags according to sampling policy below. Logs must include request_id and enriched context for at least ERROR level events.

Metrics
- HOTPATH: p50/p90/p95/p99 latency for request and token generation, tokens/sec, concurrency, error-rate (4xx/5xx).
  - SLO example (HOSTED): p95 end-to-end latency < 150ms, availability 99.95% monthly.
- COLDPATH: throughput (items/sec), job-latency percentiles, queue depth.
- CONTROL: operation-duration, success-rate, and authorization-failures. Target success-rate >= 99.9% for critical control ops.
- BATCH & BACKGROUND: job completion time, retry-count, cost-estimate.

Tracing and Sampling
- HOTPATH: adaptive sampling with baseline 5% global + 100% for traces containing errors or latency > p95 for that route. Capture full span stack for sampled traces.
- COLDPATH/BATCH: 1% sampling by default; increase to 5% during anomalies.
- CONTROL: 100% sampling for rollout/change events; record config snapshot reference.

Logs
- Minimum log level for production services: INFO.
- ERROR and WARNING logs must be forwarded to the central log pipeline with tenant_id and request_id.
- For Private deployments with compliance requirements, set log retention and masking per customer contract (see "Data Retention & Masking" table).

Telemetry Tagging and Cost Control
- Each metric submission MUST include a cost_center tag (service-owned). The metrics ingestion pipeline will bill back based on cost_center and traffic_class to compute per-tenant telemetry costs.
- To avoid unbounded metric cardinality, cardinality limits per metric name: 50 values for model_id, 200 values for route, 1000 values for tenant_id. Alert if any metric exceeds cardinality limits for > 24 hours.

Encryption and Key Management
----------------------------
In-transit
- All network traffic between external clients and api-gateway MUST use TLS 1.3.
- Internal RPCs in Hosted and Dedicated networks MUST use mTLS with X.509 issued by Redwood PKI.
- Private deployments may opt to configure customer-managed CA; document required certificate CN format: CN=redwood-internal-<service>-<cluster>.

At-rest
- Model artifacts and KV caches on disk must be encrypted with AES-256.
- Snapshot storage (S3-compatible) must store objects with server-side encryption (SSE) enabled.

Key Management
- Hosted: Redwood-managed KMS (AWS KMS / GCP KMS) with keys rotated annually by default.
- Dedicated: Option to use Redwood-managed KMS or customer-provided keys.
- Private: Must support customer-provided KMS/HSM; require explicit configuration and verification during onboarding.
- All deployments: all key usage must be auditable; log key-access events to the audit-log service (control-plane-only) with retention per contract.

Rekeying and Compromise Plan
- Rekey cadence: rotate symmetric data keys every 12 months; master key rotation can be 36 months but on-demand rotation supported.
- Compromise procedure: revoke affected key, trigger transparent re-encryption job with bounded concurrency, notify security and affected tenants within SLA (24h for sensitive leaks). See Incident Response Security runbook for step-by-step actions.

Data Retention & Masking (summary table)
- Note: retention can be overridden by legal/compliance for specific customers.

| Data Type | Default Retention | Raw/Derived | Masking/Notes |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| Raw request logs (error-level) | 365 days (Private) / 90 days (Hosted) | Raw | Must be redacted for PII prior to indexing |
| Aggregated metrics (1m) | 5 years (aggregated) | Derived | High-resolution raw (15s) kept 7 days |
| Traces | 30 days (prod) | Raw | Store full payload only for sampled traces |
| Model artifacts | Indefinite (versioned) | Raw | Access controlled by KMS |

Routing and Fallback Behavior by Class
-------------------------------------
- HOTPATH: prefer lowest-latency models in catalog; fallback chain must be declared in model policy metadata (primary -> warmed-quantized -> small-variant). Fallback must preserve semantics and be transparent in logs.
- COLDPATH/BATCH: queue and batch aggressively; expose estimated start-time to caller header X-Redwood-Estimate-Start.
- CONTROL: must not be routed through tenant noisy-hosted clusters. Control ops are isolated to control-plane nodes.

Rollout and Change Management
----------------------------
All changes that affect traffic classification, telemetry shape, encryption, or header contracts MUST follow this rollout: 
1. Design review (-2 weeks): architectural review board signoff; include performance model and cardinality impact analysis.
2. Dev/Staging: apply new headers and telemetry to staging clusters; run synthetic load test with noise injection.
3. Canary: 5% traffic for 48 hours. Verify metrics: no >10% increase in p95 or >2x baseline error-rate.
4. Ramp: 25% for 72 hours, 50% for 48 hours, then 100% if stable.
5. Post-rollout: 30-day observation with weekly summary and rollback window of 14 days.

Operational Playbook (short)
---------------------------
- If cardinality alert fires: enable short-term metric aggregation and engage runtime-observability engineer. Steps:
  1. Identify offending label values via metrics-explorer.
  2. Apply tag suppression on router if noise is client generated.
  3. Rollback configuration change if introduced by the last deployment.

- If encryption audit fails (e.g., expired cert/missing key access): switch to backup key, notify security, open P1 incident.

Ownership and Contacts
----------------------
- Runtime Observability Team (primary): runtime-observability@redwood.ai. Owners: Marcus Li (lead), Ana Gómez.
- Infra-SRE (secondary): infra-sre@redwood.ai. Owner: Ethan Park.
- Security (review/incident): security@redwood.ai. Owner: Daniel Wu.

Examples and Snippets
---------------------
Telemetry emitter example (pseudo-YAML) for HOTPATH:

metrics:
  - name: request_latency_ms
    labels: [service, route, model_id, traffic_class, tenant_id]
    buckets: [1,5,10,25,50,100,250,500,1000]

Router policy example (JSON)
{
  "route": "/v1/generate",
  "traffic_class": "HOTPATH",
  "fallback_chain": ["gptx-13b", "gptx-6b-quant", "gptx-3b-small"],
  "telemetry_tags": {"cost_center":"inference-hosted"}
}

Migration Checklist for Existing Services
---------------------------------------
- Add X-Redwood-Traffic-Class header at service ingress.
- Emit tenant_id and traffic_class in metrics and traces.
- Verify mTLS between runtime components in staging.
- Run cardinality scanner and fix high-cardinality labels before canary.

Open Questions and Exceptions
----------------------------
- Special-case for low-throughput, privacy-sensitive tenants in Hosted: allow per-tenant retention overrides but require documented approval from Security and Legal.
- Legacy SDKs that cannot set headers: router will attempt to classify by route heuristics; teams must document heuristics and migrate SDKs within 6 months.

Related Documents
-----------------
- Telemetry ingestion and retention guidelines (see related_pages).
- ADR: Model slicing and fallback routing (see related_pages).

Revision History
----------------
- 2026-01-10: Created by Priya Kapoor (initial draft).
- 2026-02-18: Published after reviews by Marcus Li, Ana Gómez, and Security (updates to sampling and retention tables).

Approval
--------
Approved by: Architecture Review Board (signature stored in review artifacts).

source.title: Cost Model API Guidelines (API Standards Addendum)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.body:
## Purpose
This page is an addendum to Redwood’s general API standards, defining additional requirements for the **Cost Model API**. The Cost Model API is consumed by Redwood Console and Redwood Optimize and is also used to generate finance/unit-economics primitives.

The goals of these guidelines are to:
- Keep cost outputs **consistent and explainable** across product surfaces.
- Ensure **bounded** request/response sizes and compute cost in the presence of high-cardinality telemetry.
- Provide a stable path for **schema and semantic evolution** (versioning + deprecation).
- Define explicit expectations around **determinism**, **caching**, and **error handling**.

## Scope
These rules apply to endpoints under the Cost Model service, including (names illustrative):
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/estimate`
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/breakdown`
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/what-if`

They apply to both the service and the shared client library.

Non-goal: This document does not define customer-facing pricing or the billing system of record. It defines standardized primitives for internal products and reporting.

---

## 1) Naming and resource conventions
### 1.1 Endpoint naming
- Use **nouns for resources** and **verbs only when the operation is not CRUD**.
- Prefer **operation-style endpoints** for compute-heavy workflows:
  - `estimate` (per-request or per-workload cost estimate)
  - `breakdown` (canonical breakdown for display/reporting)
  - `what-if` (projection under a proposed config delta)

Examples:
- ✅ `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/estimate`
- ✅ `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/what-if`
- ❌ `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/getCostForConsole` (product-specific naming)

### 1.2 Field naming
- JSON field names use `snake_case`.
- IDs use `_id` suffix; version markers use `_version` suffix.
- Timestamps are RFC3339 strings (`2026-02-17T19:22:11Z`) unless otherwise specified.

### 1.3 Canonical taxonomy fields
The breakdown taxonomy must align with ADR-022:
- Stages: `prefill`, `decode`
- Attribution categories (high-level): `request_attributable`, `shared_overhead`
- Explicit modeling of: `cache_hit`, `cache_miss`, `batch_efficiency`, `quantization_delta` (as applicable)

The service must not invent product-specific categories. If Console or Optimize needs a new slice, it must be proposed as a taxonomy extension (see Versioning).

---

## 2) Bounded requests and responses (pagination + limits)
Cost endpoints are frequently called on hot paths (Console dashboards) and can be used in iterative loops (Optimize). **All endpoints must be explicitly bounded.**

### 2.1 Hard limits (service-side)
The service must enforce maximums and return a structured error when exceeded:
- Maximum time range for aggregated queries (e.g., breakdown by time): **31 days**.
- Maximum number of group-by dimensions: **3**.
- Maximum cardinality per response for any group-by dimension: **2,000** distinct values (after filtering).
- Maximum number of total rows/entries returned: **10,000**.
- Maximum request payload size: **256 KB**.

If a request would exceed bounds, return `400` with `error_code=REQUEST_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED` and include guidance (see Error Codes).

### 2.2 Pagination rules
When returning lists of rows/entries:
- Use **cursor-based pagination**.
- Response includes `next_page_token` (empty when complete).
- Page size is caller-controlled but capped; recommend default `page_size=500` and maximum `page_size=2000`.

If a caller requests `page_size` above the cap, clamp to the max and emit a warning field/message.

### 2.3 Aggregation defaults and safe fallbacks
For aggregation endpoints:
- Default aggregation windows must be documented and stable (e.g., hourly buckets for >24h ranges).
- When high cardinality is detected:
  - Prefer truncating with explicit `is_truncated=true` + `truncation_reason` over silently dropping rows.
  - Provide a recommended remediation message (e.g., “add filter on model_id”, “reduce time range”).

### 2.4 Product-facing requirements
- Console: avoid per-request fanout. Prefer pre-aggregated breakdown calls with limited dimensions.
- Optimize: prefer workload summary inputs over raw request lists. Any “N-example replay” workflows must cap N and be behind internal guardrails.

---

## 3) Determinism and reproducibility expectations
The Cost Model API is used for:
- User-facing cost breakdown (Console)
- ROI projections (Optimize)
- Internal reporting windows (Finance)

Therefore the API must explicitly define when responses are deterministic.

### 3.1 Determinism definition
A response is considered **deterministic** if the same request payload and the same referenced snapshots yield the same results.

To achieve this, cost computations must reference explicit snapshots/versions:
- `cost_model_spec_version` (semantic version)
- `pricing_snapshot_id` (or `rate_card_version`) for cost rates
- `model_catalog_snapshot_id` for model metadata
- `telemetry_watermark` for aggregated/observed inputs

If the request omits snapshot identifiers, the service may use “latest”, but the response must echo the resolved values in a stable field so callers can audit.

### 3.2 Required response echo fields
Every successful response must include, at minimum, the following top-level metadata fields (names may vary but semantics must match):
- `resolved_cost_model_spec_version`
- `resolved_pricing_snapshot_id`
- `resolved_model_catalog_snapshot_id`
- `resolved_telemetry_watermark`
- `computed_at`

### 3.3 Handling telemetry lag
When results depend on telemetry that may be incomplete:
- The service must expose a `telemetry_completeness` indicator (`complete` | `partial` | `unknown`) and a watermark timestamp.
- If telemetry is partial and the endpoint is used for finance-aligned reporting, the service should return `206 Partial Content` (HTTP) or `200` with an explicit `partial=true` flag (choose one convention per endpoint and document it).

### 3.4 Floating-point and rounding rules
- All currency values must be returned as **decimal strings** (not binary floats) with a documented precision (e.g., 1/10,000 USD).
- Token and unit counts are integers.
- Percentages/rates returned as decimal strings with explicit units.

### 3.5 Idempotency
All POST endpoints must be safe to retry.
- Support `Idempotency-Key` header for endpoints that may write audit logs or cache entries.
- Duplicate requests with the same key within the TTL must return the same response body (or a stable subset).

---

## 4) Error model and status codes
### 4.1 Error response shape
All errors must return:
- `error_code` (stable enum string)
- `message` (human-readable)
- `request_id` (for support/oncall)
- `details` (optional, but must be bounded and not include customer data)

### 4.2 HTTP status mapping
- `400 Bad Request`: invalid input, schema validation failure, bounds exceeded
- `401 Unauthorized`: missing/invalid service identity
- `403 Forbidden`: authenticated but not permitted for tenant/project
- `404 Not Found`: unknown model_id / snapshot_id (only when the identifier is client-provided)
- `409 Conflict`: version negotiation conflict (e.g., requested spec version not supported)
- `422 Unprocessable Entity`: semantically invalid combination (e.g., what-if delta not applicable to deployment mode)
- `429 Too Many Requests`: rate limited (include retry guidance)
- `499 Client Closed Request` (if supported): client cancelled
- `500 Internal Server Error`: unexpected
- `503 Service Unavailable`: dependency down or overload

### 4.3 Standard error codes (minimum set)
- `INVALID_ARGUMENT`
- `SCHEMA_VALIDATION_FAILED`
- `UNSUPPORTED_SPEC_VERSION`
- `REQUEST_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED`
- `UNKNOWN_MODEL`
- `UNKNOWN_SNAPSHOT`
- `TENANT_ACCESS_DENIED`
- `RATE_LIMITED`
- `DEPENDENCY_UNAVAILABLE`
- `COMPUTE_BUDGET_EXCEEDED`
- `TELEMETRY_INCOMPLETE`

Notes:
- `COMPUTE_BUDGET_EXCEEDED` is used when the service rejects/aborts due to internal guardrails (time or cost).
- `TELEMETRY_INCOMPLETE` should be used when completeness is required by the endpoint contract (e.g., a “finance-grade” mode).

### 4.4 Retry semantics
The client library must implement conservative retries:
- Retry on: `429`, `503`, and transport-level timeouts.
- Do not retry on: `400`, `403`, `404`, `409`, `422`.
- Expose response headers/fields for `retry_after_ms` when rate limited.

---

## 5) Versioning, compatibility, and deprecation
### 5.1 Two layers of versioning
Cost Model has two separate but related versions:
1) **API version** (path-based): `/v1/` controls endpoint shape and protocol behavior.
2) **Spec version** (payload-driven): `cost_model_spec_version` controls schema + semantics of cost computation.

Both must be present in documentation; do not conflate them.

### 5.2 Backward compatibility rules
- Additive changes (new optional fields) are allowed within `/v1/`.
- Removing fields, changing meaning, or changing default rounding is **not allowed** within the same spec minor version.
- Any semantic change that could affect Console displays or finance reporting must bump the **spec minor** (or major) version and be announced.

### 5.3 Version negotiation
- Requests may provide `requested_cost_model_spec_version` (exact) OR `min_cost_model_spec_version` + `max_cost_model_spec_version`.
- The service must respond with the `resolved_cost_model_spec_version`.
- If no compatible version exists, return `409` with `error_code=UNSUPPORTED_SPEC_VERSION`.

### 5.4 Deprecation policy
- Deprecations must be announced with at least:
  - **60 days** notice for internal callers
  - **90 days** notice if finance reporting depends on the field/behavior
- A deprecated field must continue to be populated for the deprecation window and must be marked in docs with:
  - deprecation date
  - removal date
  - replacement field

### 5.5 Reporting windows and “frozen semantics”
For finance-aligned reporting:
- The service must support querying with an explicit `pricing_snapshot_id` and `cost_model_spec_version` to reproduce historical periods.
- “Latest” defaults are allowed for interactive product UX, but finance jobs must pin versions.

---

## 6) Security, tenancy, and data handling
- All endpoints must enforce tenant scoping (org/project/environment).
- Responses must avoid leaking cross-tenant metadata (model catalog entries must be filtered to what the caller is allowed to reference).
- Do not return raw prompt content or request payloads. Where request-derived debugging is necessary, return hashed identifiers.

---

## 7) Observability requirements (minimum)
Every endpoint must emit:
- Request counters by: endpoint, status code, `resolved_cost_model_spec_version`
- Latency distributions (p50/p95/p99)
- Truncation metrics: count of truncated responses + reason
- Drift/correctness signals where applicable (see drift SLI doc): predicted vs observed deltas aggregated over time

Responses must include a `request_id` that maps 1:1 to server logs/traces.

---

## 8) Examples
### 8.1 Version negotiation (request)
Headers:
- `X-Redwood-Tenant: org_123`
- `X-Redwood-Project: proj_456`

Body (illustrative):
- request provides `min_cost_model_spec_version=1.1.0` and `max_cost_model_spec_version=1.2.0`
- response echoes `resolved_cost_model_spec_version=1.2.0`

### 8.2 Bounds exceeded (error)
HTTP `400`:
- `error_code=REQUEST_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED`
- `message` includes: “group_by dimensions=5 exceeds max=3; reduce dimensions or add filters”
- `request_id` included for oncall correlation

---

## Ownership and change process
Owner: Platform Control Plane (service/API) with standards review by Architecture & Standards.

Any change that affects taxonomy, rounding, or overhead allocation requires:
- An ADR update (or new ADR) when semantics change materially
- Review by Finance stakeholder for reporting impact
- A migration note for Console/Optimize

If you need an exception to any guideline here, open a review in the #architecture channel and link the proposed change + impact analysis.

- The service must expose a `telemetry_completeness` indicator (`complete` | `partial` | `unknown`) and a watermark timestamp.
- If telemetry is partial and the endpoint is used for finance-aligned reporting, the service should return `206 Partial Content` (HTTP) or `200` with an explicit `partial=true` flag (choose one convention per endpoint and document it).

### 3.4 Floating-point and rounding rules
- All currency values must be returned as **decimal strings** (not binary floats) with a documented precision (e.g., 1/10,000 USD).
- Token and unit counts are integers.
- Percentages/rates returned as decimal strings with explicit units.

### 3.5 Idempotency
All POST endpoints must be safe to retry.
- Support `Idempotency-Key` header for endpoints that may write audit logs or cache entries.
- Duplicate requests with the same key within the TTL must return the same response body (or a stable subset).

---

## 4) Error model and status codes
### 4.1 Error response shape
All errors must return:
- `error_code` (stable enum string)
- `message` (human-readable)
- `request_id` (for support/oncall)
- `details` (optional, but must be bounded and not include customer data)

### 4.2 HTTP status mapping
- `400 Bad Request`: invalid input, schema validation failure, bounds exceeded
- `401 Unauthorized`: missing/invalid service identity
- `403 Forbidden`: authenticated but not permitted for tenant/project
- `404 Not Found`: unknown model_id / snapshot_id (only when the identifier is client-provided)
- `409 Conflict`: version negotiation conflict (e.g., requested spec version not supported)
- `422 Unprocessable Entity`: semantically invalid combination (e.g., what-if delta not applicable to deployment mode)
- `429 Too Many Requests`: rate limited (include retry guidance)
- `499 Client Closed Request` (if supported): client cancelled
- `500 Internal Server Error`: unexpected
- `503 Service Unavailable`: dependency down or overload

### 4.3 Standard error codes (minimum set)
- `INVALID_ARGUMENT`
- `SCHEMA_VALIDATION_FAILED`
- `UNSUPPORTED_SPEC_VERSION`
- `REQUEST_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED`
- `UNKNOWN_MODEL`
- `UNKNOWN_SNAPSHOT`
- `TENANT_ACCESS_DENIED`
- `RATE_LIMITED`
- `DEPENDENCY_UNAVAILABLE`
- `COMPUTE_BUDGET_EXCEEDED`
- `TELEMETRY_INCOMPLETE`

Notes:
- `COMPUTE_BUDGET_EXCEEDED` is used when the service rejects/aborts due to internal guardrails (time or cost).
- `TELEMETRY_INCOMPLETE` should be used when completeness is required by the endpoint contract (e.g., a “finance-grade” mode).

### 4.4 Retry semantics
The client library must implement conservative retries:
- Retry on: `429`, `503`, and transport-level timeouts.
- Do not retry on: `400`, `403`, `404`, `409`, `422`.
- Expose response headers/fields for `retry_after_ms` when rate limited.

---

## 5) Versioning, compatibility, and deprecation
### 5.1 Two layers of versioning
Cost Model has two separate but related versions:
1) **API version** (path-based): `/v1/` controls endpoint shape and protocol behavior.
2) **Spec version** (payload-driven): `cost_model_spec_version` controls schema + semantics of cost computation.

Both must be present in documentation; do not conflate them.

### 5.2 Backward compatibility rules
- Additive changes (new optional fields) are allowed within `/v1/`.
- Removing fields, changing meaning, or changing default rounding is **not allowed** within the same spec minor version.
- Any semantic change that could affect Console displays or finance reporting must bump the **spec minor** (or major) version and be announced.

### 5.3 Version negotiation
- Requests may provide `requested_cost_model_spec_version` (exact) OR `min_cost_model_spec_version` + `max_cost_model_spec_version`.
- The service must respond with the `resolved_cost_model_spec_version`.
- If no compatible version exists, return `409` with `error_code=UNSUPPORTED_SPEC_VERSION`.

### 5.4 Deprecation policy
- Deprecations must be announced with at least:
  - **60 days** notice for internal callers
  - **90 days** notice if finance reporting depends on the field/behavior
- A deprecated field must continue to be populated for the deprecation window and must be marked in docs with:
  - deprecation date
  - removal date
  - replacement field

### 5.5 Reporting windows and “frozen semantics”
For finance-aligned reporting:
- The service must support querying with an explicit `pricing_snapshot_id` and `cost_model_spec_version` to reproduce historical periods.
- “Latest” defaults are allowed for interactive product UX, but finance jobs must pin versions.

---

## 6) Security, tenancy, and data handling
- All endpoints must enforce tenant scoping (org/project/environment).
- Responses must avoid leaking cross-tenant metadata (model catalog entries must be filtered to what the caller is allowed to reference).
- Do not return raw prompt content or request payloads. Where request-derived debugging is necessary, return hashed identifiers.

---

## 7) Observability requirements (minimum)
Every endpoint must emit:
- Request counters by: endpoint, status code, `resolved_cost_model_spec_version`
- Latency distributions (p50/p95/p99)
- Truncation metrics: count of truncated responses + reason
- Drift/correctness signals where applicable (see drift SLI doc): predicted vs observed deltas aggregated over time

Responses must include a `request_id` that maps 1:1 to server logs/traces.

---

## 8) Examples
### 8.1 Version negotiation (request)
Headers:
- `X-Redwood-Tenant: org_123`
- `X-Redwood-Project: proj_456`

Body (illustrative):
- request provides `min_cost_model_spec_version=1.1.0` and `max_cost_model_spec_version=1.2.0`
- response echoes `resolved_cost_model_spec_version=1.2.0`

### 8.2 Bounds exceeded (error)
HTTP `400`:
- `error_code=REQUEST_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED`
- `message` includes: “group_by dimensions=5 exceeds max=3; reduce dimensions or add filters”
- `request_id` included for oncall correlation

---

## Ownership and change process
Owner: Platform Control Plane (service/API) with standards review by Architecture & Standards.

Any change that affects taxonomy, rounding, or overhead allocation requires:
- An ADR update (or new ADR) when semantics change materially
- Review by Finance stakeholder for reporting impact
- A migration note for Console/Optimize

If you need an exception to any guideline here, open a review in the #architecture channel and link the proposed change + impact analysis.
text:
## Purpose
This page is an addendum to Redwood’s general API standards, defining additional requirements for the **Cost Model API**. The Cost Model API is consumed by Redwood Console and Redwood Optimize and is also used to generate finance/unit-economics primitives.

The goals of these guidelines are to:
- Keep cost outputs **consistent and explainable** across product surfaces.
- Ensure **bounded** request/response sizes and compute cost in the presence of high-cardinality telemetry.
- Provide a stable path for **schema and semantic evolution** (versioning + deprecation).
- Define explicit expectations around **determinism**, **caching**, and **error handling**.

## Scope
These rules apply to endpoints under the Cost Model service, including (names illustrative):
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/estimate`
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/breakdown`
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/what-if`

They apply to both the service and the shared client library.

Non-goal: This document does not define customer-facing pricing or the billing system of record. It defines standardized primitives for internal products and reporting.

---

## 1) Naming and resource conventions
### 1.1 Endpoint naming
- Use **nouns for resources** and **verbs only when the operation is not CRUD**.
- Prefer **operation-style endpoints** for compute-heavy workflows:
  - `estimate` (per-request or per-workload cost estimate)
  - `breakdown` (canonical breakdown for display/reporting)
  - `what-if` (projection under a proposed config delta)

Examples:
- ✅ `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/estimate`
- ✅ `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/what-if`
- ❌ `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/getCostForConsole` (product-specific naming)

### 1.2 Field naming
- JSON field names use `snake_case`.
- IDs use `_id` suffix; version markers use `_version` suffix.
- Timestamps are RFC3339 strings (`2026-02-17T19:22:11Z`) unless otherwise specified.

### 1.3 Canonical taxonomy fields
The breakdown taxonomy must align with ADR-022:
- Stages: `prefill`, `decode`
- Attribution categories (high-level): `request_attributable`, `shared_overhead`
- Explicit modeling of: `cache_hit`, `cache_miss`, `batch_efficiency`, `quantization_delta` (as applicable)

The service must not invent product-specific categories. If Console or Optimize needs a new slice, it must be proposed as a taxonomy extension (see Versioning).

---

## 2) Bounded requests and responses (pagination + limits)
Cost endpoints are frequently called on hot paths (Console dashboards) and can be used in iterative loops (Optimize). **All endpoints must be explicitly bounded.**

### 2.1 Hard limits (service-side)
The service must enforce maximums and return a structured error when exceeded:
- Maximum time range for aggregated queries (e.g., breakdown by time): **31 days**.
- Maximum number of group-by dimensions: **3**.
- Maximum cardinality per response for any group-by dimension: **2,000** distinct values (after filtering).
- Maximum number of total rows/entries returned: **10,000**.
- Maximum request payload size: **256 KB**.

If a request would exceed bounds, return `400` with `error_code=REQUEST_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED` and include guidance (see Error Codes).

### 2.2 Pagination rules
When returning lists of rows/entries:
- Use **cursor-based pagination**.
- Response includes `next_page_token` (empty when complete).
- Page size is caller-controlled but capped; recommend default `page_size=500` and maximum `page_size=2000`.

If a caller requests `page_size` above the cap, clamp to the max and emit a warning field/message.

### 2.3 Aggregation defaults and safe fallbacks
For aggregation endpoints:
- Default aggregation windows must be documented and stable (e.g., hourly buckets for >24h ranges).
- When high cardinality is detected:
  - Prefer truncating with explicit `is_truncated=true` + `truncation_reason` over silently dropping rows.
  - Provide a recommended remediation message (e.g., “add filter on model_id”, “reduce time range”).

### 2.4 Product-facing requirements
- Console: avoid per-request fanout. Prefer pre-aggregated breakdown calls with limited dimensions.
- Optimize: prefer workload summary inputs over raw request lists. Any “N-example replay” workflows must cap N and be behind internal guardrails.

---

## 3) Determinism and reproducibility expectations
The Cost Model API is used for:
- User-facing cost breakdown (Console)
- ROI projections (Optimize)
- Internal reporting windows (Finance)

Therefore the API must explicitly define when responses are deterministic.

### 3.1 Determinism definition
A response is considered **deterministic** if the same request payload and the same referenced snapshots yield the same results.

To achieve this, cost computations must reference explicit snapshots/versions:
- `cost_model_spec_version` (semantic version)
- `pricing_snapshot_id` (or `rate_card_version`) for cost rates
- `model_catalog_snapshot_id` for model metadata
- `telemetry_watermark` for aggregated/observed inputs

If the request omits snapshot identifiers, the service may use “latest”, but the response must echo the resolved values in a stable field so callers can audit.

### 3.2 Required response echo fields
Every successful response must include, at minimum, the following top-level metadata fields (names may vary but semantics must match):
- `resolved_cost_model_spec_version`
- `resolved_pricing_snapshot_id`
- `resolved_model_catalog_snapshot_id`
- `resolved_telemetry_watermark`
- `computed_at`

### 3.3 Handling telemetry lag
When results depend on telemetry that may be incomplete:
- The service must expose a `telemetry_completeness` indicator (`complete` | `partial` | `unknown`) and a watermark timestamp.
- If telemetry is partial and the endpoint is used for finance-aligned reporting, the service should return `206 Partial Content` (HTTP) or `200` with an explicit `partial=true` flag (choose one convention per endpoint and document it).

### 3.4 Floating-point and rounding rules
- All currency values must be returned as **decimal strings** (not binary floats) with a documented precision (e.g., 1/10,000 USD).
- Token and unit counts are integers.
- Percentages/rates returned as decimal strings with explicit units.

### 3.5 Idempotency
All POST endpoints must be safe to retry.
- Support `Idempotency-Key` header for endpoints that may write audit logs or cache entries.
- Duplicate requests with the same key within the TTL must return the same response body (or a stable subset).

---

## 4) Error model and status codes
### 4.1 Error response shape
All errors must return:
- `error_code` (stable enum string)
- `message` (human-readable)
- `request_id` (for support/oncall)
- `details` (optional, but must be bounded and not include customer data)

### 4.2 HTTP status mapping
- `400 Bad Request`: invalid input, schema validation failure, bounds exceeded
- `401 Unauthorized`: missing/invalid service identity
- `403 Forbidden`: authenticated but not permitted for tenant/project
- `404 Not Found`: unknown model_id / snapshot_id (only when the identifier is client-provided)
- `409 Conflict`: version negotiation conflict (e.g., requested spec version not supported)
- `422 Unprocessable Entity`: semantically invalid combination (e.g., what-if delta not applicable to deployment mode)
- `429 Too Many Requests`: rate limited (include retry guidance)
- `499 Client Closed Request` (if supported): client cancelled
- `500 Internal Server Error`: unexpected
- `503 Service Unavailable`: dependency down or overload

### 4.3 Standard error codes (minimum set)
- `INVALID_ARGUMENT`
- `SCHEMA_VALIDATION_FAILED`
- `UNSUPPORTED_SPEC_VERSION`
- `REQUEST_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED`
- `UNKNOWN_MODEL`
- `UNKNOWN_SNAPSHOT`
- `TENANT_ACCESS_DENIED`
- `RATE_LIMITED`
- `DEPENDENCY_UNAVAILABLE`
- `COMPUTE_BUDGET_EXCEEDED`
- `TELEMETRY_INCOMPLETE`

Notes:
- `COMPUTE_BUDGET_EXCEEDED` is used when the service rejects/aborts due to internal guardrails (time or cost).
- `TELEMETRY_INCOMPLETE` should be used when completeness is required by the endpoint contract (e.g., a “finance-grade” mode).

### 4.4 Retry semantics
The client library must implement conservative retries:
- Retry on: `429`, `503`, and transport-level timeouts.
- Do not retry on: `400`, `403`, `404`, `409`, `422`.
- Expose response headers/fields for `retry_after_ms` when rate limited.

---

## 5) Versioning, compatibility, and deprecation
### 5.1 Two layers of versioning
Cost Model has two separate but related versions:
1) **API version** (path-based): `/v1/` controls endpoint shape and protocol behavior.
2) **Spec version** (payload-driven): `cost_model_spec_version` controls schema + semantics of cost computation.

Both must be present in documentation; do not conflate them.

### 5.2 Backward compatibility rules
- Additive changes (new optional fields) are allowed within `/v1/`.
- Removing fields, changing meaning, or changing default rounding is **not allowed** within the same spec minor version.
- Any semantic change that could affect Console displays or finance reporting must bump the **spec minor** (or major) version and be announced.

### 5.3 Version negotiation
- Requests may provide `requested_cost_model_spec_version` (exact) OR `min_cost_model_spec_version` + `max_cost_model_spec_version`.
- The service must respond with the `resolved_cost_model_spec_version`.
- If no compatible version exists, return `409` with `error_code=UNSUPPORTED_SPEC_VERSION`.

### 5.4 Deprecation policy
- Deprecations must be announced with at least:
  - **60 days** notice for internal callers
  - **90 days** notice if finance reporting depends on the field/behavior
- A deprecated field must continue to be populated for the deprecation window and must be marked in docs with:
  - deprecation date
  - removal date
  - replacement field

### 5.5 Reporting windows and “frozen semantics”
For finance-aligned reporting:
- The service must support querying with an explicit `pricing_snapshot_id` and `cost_model_spec_version` to reproduce historical periods.
- “Latest” defaults are allowed for interactive product UX, but finance jobs must pin versions.

---

## 6) Security, tenancy, and data handling
- All endpoints must enforce tenant scoping (org/project/environment).
- Responses must avoid leaking cross-tenant metadata (model catalog entries must be filtered to what the caller is allowed to reference).
- Do not return raw prompt content or request payloads. Where request-derived debugging is necessary, return hashed identifiers.

---

## 7) Observability requirements (minimum)
Every endpoint must emit:
- Request counters by: endpoint, status code, `resolved_cost_model_spec_version`
- Latency distributions (p50/p95/p99)
- Truncation metrics: count of truncated responses + reason
- Drift/correctness signals where applicable (see drift SLI doc): predicted vs observed deltas aggregated over time

Responses must include a `request_id` that maps 1:1 to server logs/traces.

---

## 8) Examples
### 8.1 Version negotiation (request)
Headers:
- `X-Redwood-Tenant: org_123`
- `X-Redwood-Project: proj_456`

Body (illustrative):
- request provides `min_cost_model_spec_version=1.1.0` and `max_cost_model_spec_version=1.2.0`
- response echoes `resolved_cost_model_spec_version=1.2.0`

### 8.2 Bounds exceeded (error)
HTTP `400`:
- `error_code=REQUEST_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED`
- `message` includes: “group_by dimensions=5 exceeds max=3; reduce dimensions or add filters”
- `request_id` included for oncall correlation

---

## Ownership and change process
Owner: Platform Control Plane (service/API) with standards review by Architecture & Standards.

Any change that affects taxonomy, rounding, or overhead allocation requires:
- An ADR update (or new ADR) when semantics change materially

source.title: ADR-017: Kernel Auto-Selection v2 — Feature Set, Rule-Based Decisioning, and Fallback Policy
source.author: Noah Patel
source.content:
## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind feature gates; rollout in progress for Runtime 1.21).

## Context
Kernel auto-selection v1 relies on a small set of heuristics and a mostly global “best default” per model family. This has led to:
- Tail latency spikes (p95/p99) when long-context or mixed workloads are misclassified.
- Throughput instability across heterogeneous fleets (A100/H100/L40S) where the optimal kernel differs by SKU.
- Slow incident response when a kernel becomes unstable on a specific driver/CUDA combo; v1 lacked a consistent, centrally-managed fallback and denylist policy.

Kernel auto-selection v2 needs to:
- Improve latency and throughput stability with minimal runtime overhead.
- Preserve **correctness** and **determinism** (selection is replayable given the same inputs).
- Support safe rollout and rapid rollback with per-model and per-SKU controls.
- Be explainable and operationally debuggable (oncall can understand why a kernel was chosen and how to override it).

This ADR records (1) the chosen selection approach, (2) the feature set available to the selector, and (3) the explicit fallback policy.

## Decision
### 1) Selection approach: rule-based decisioning (not learned scoring)
We will implement kernel auto-selection v2 as a **deterministic, rule-based decision system** with a small, ordered set of candidate selection rules keyed on:
- Model family / architecture
- Sequence-length regime (prefill vs decode characteristics; context length buckets)
- GPU SKU / capability profile

The selector may compute a simple **rule score / ordering** internally, but it must be interpretable, deterministic, and driven by explicit config + compiled-in rules. We will **not** deploy a learned model (ML-based scoring) for kernel selection in v2.

**Rationale**
- **Explainability and debugging:** Oncall and customers (Dedicated/Private) require a clear mapping from inputs → decision.
- **Rollback safety:** Rule changes can be pinned, gated, and rolled back quickly. Learned scoring introduces opaque regressions and heavier offline evaluation requirements.
- **Determinism guarantees:** A learned system would likely depend on richer/continuous signals and could introduce nondeterminism via feature drift, sampling, or dependency updates.
- **Operational constraints:** Private deployments may not be able to ship frequent model/scoring updates; rules + config can be managed more conservatively.

### 2) Feature set used by v2
The selector’s decision inputs are limited to the following feature categories. Any future features must be added via a new ADR or an amendment to this one.

#### A. Model family / architecture signals (required)
The selector will classify the request into a normalized model family tag based on the loaded model metadata:
- Examples: `llama`, `mistral`, `qwen`, `gemma` (extensible)
- Derived from: model config (architecture name), tokenizer/model repo tags, and Redwood-internal model registry annotations.
- Versioning: model family tag is computed deterministically from model metadata and pinned to the loaded model version.

Notes:
- Variants (e.g., instruct vs base, different rope scaling) may map to the same family but can be further differentiated via optional sub-tags when needed for kernel compatibility.

#### B. Sequence-length regime signals (required)
The selector will bucket each request into a small set of context-length / regime classes:
- `short_ctx`: typical prompts with small prefill (low total input tokens)
- `mid_ctx`
- `long_ctx`: long-context prompts where prefill dominates and tail risks are highest

Additionally, the selector may use a derived “prefill/decode dominance hint” if available (e.g., expected decode length from request `max_tokens` and historic workload hints).

Constraints:
- Buckets must be **stable** (explicit thresholds in config; no adaptive bucketing at request time).
- Bucket boundaries must be tracked and versioned to preserve replayability in incident analysis.

#### C. GPU SKU / capability signals (required)
The selector will use a normalized GPU SKU identifier and a small derived capability profile.
- SKU examples: `nvidia-a100-80gb`, `nvidia-h100-80gb`, `nvidia-l40s-48gb`
- Capability profile may include: `sm_version`, `hbm_gb`, `supports_fp8`, `supports_bf16`, and driver/CUDA compatibility flags.

Normalization requirements:
- Cloud instance naming, MIG variants, and vendor strings must map to a canonical SKU in the runtime.
- MIG instances must be treated as separate capacity profiles if kernel behavior differs materially.

#### D. Optional: scheduler-provided workload hints (allowed, gated)
The scheduler may pass a coarse “workload distribution hint” (e.g., observed seq-length distribution for the pool/route) to stabilize selection for mixed workloads.
- This signal is **optional** and must be behind a feature flag.
- The selector must remain deterministic: for replay, the hint must be logged with the selection event when used.

#### E. Explicitly excluded features (v2)
To reduce nondeterminism and complexity, v2 will not use:
- Live GPU utilization / queue depth as selection inputs (belongs to scheduling/routing).
- Per-request performance feedback loops (online learning).
- Request content features (PII/security concerns; also unstable).

### 3) Fallback policy (missing / unstable kernels)
The selector must always produce a safe kernel choice or fail closed with a clear error classification. Fallback behavior is deterministic and ordered.

#### Definitions
- **Candidate set:** all kernels that are (a) compatible with the model family, (b) compiled/available for the GPU SKU, and (c) not blocked by policy.
- **Policy blocks:** denylist entries at any of the following levels:
  - (kernel, GPU SKU)
  - (kernel, driver/CUDA range)
  - (kernel, model family)
  - (model, GPU SKU) for v2 enablement

#### Fallback precedence (ordered)
When the “preferred” kernel for a (model family, seq bucket, GPU SKU) is not eligible:
1. **Same family, same regime, next-best kernel for that GPU SKU** (as defined by the rule table ordering).
2. **Same family, conservative kernel for that GPU SKU** (known-stable baseline; may trade throughput for predictable tail).
3. **Cross-SKU safe baseline kernel** (portable kernel variant known to be correct across SKUs; only if compatibility is explicitly certified).
4. **Kernel-selection v1 behavior** (only if v2 is enabled but v2 cannot produce an eligible candidate; emits a telemetry event `fallback_to_v1`).
5. **Fail closed** with error `KERNEL_SELECTION_NO_ELIGIBLE_CANDIDATE` if neither v2 nor v1 can safely select a kernel.

Rules:
- Fallbacks 1–3 must not change numerics beyond the established tolerance for that model + dtype configuration.
- Any fallback to v1 or fail-closed is considered a “selection degradation” and is surfaced in rollout dashboards.

#### Unstable kernel handling
If a kernel is observed unstable (crashes, incorrect outputs, or large tail regressions) on a specific SKU/driver combination:
- The immediate mitigation is to add a **denylist entry** at the narrowest safe scope (prefer (kernel, driver/CUDA range, SKU)).
- If needed, also disable v2 for the affected (model, SKU) pair to avoid repeated fallback churn.
- Denylist updates must be hot-reloadable in Hosted and Dedicated control planes; for Private, they are delivered via the approved config update mechanism.

#### Guardrail: driver/CUDA compatibility
If the runtime detects a GPU driver/CUDA version in a known-unstable range for v2 selection (or for specific kernels), v2 must:
- Either preemptively disable the affected kernels via denylist rules, or
- Disable v2 for that environment and fall back to v1.

## Operational implications
### Configuration and controls
v2 must support the following control planes:
- **Feature gate:** `kernel_selection_v2_enabled` at environment/region/pool scope.
- **Allowlist/denylist:** per-model and per-SKU enablement gates.
- **Override:** runtime flag to force a kernel (break-glass), and/or pin kernel choice at route/model scope for customer escalations.

### Telemetry requirements (summary)
Every selection decision must emit a structured event (sampled as needed) that includes:
- Selected kernel ID + version
- Candidate set (or candidate count + top-N IDs, depending on sampling/cardinality limits)
- Model family tag, model version
- Seq bucket and relevant thresholds version
- GPU SKU normalized string + driver/CUDA versions
- Any applied fallback reason codes (e.g., `DENYLISTED_KERNEL`, `MISSING_BINARY`, `UNSUPPORTED_SKU`, `DRIVER_GUARDRAIL`, `FALLBACK_TO_V1`)

## Alternatives considered
### A) Learned scoring model (offline trained)
Pros:
- Potentially captures complex interactions across model/seq/SKU dimensions.

Cons (rejected):
- Harder to explain and debug in incident response.
- Requires a robust offline training + validation pipeline and frequent refresh.
- Increases rollout risk and complicates deterministic replay.

### B) Pure static mapping table (no rules)
Pros:
- Simple implementation.

Cons (rejected):
- Doesn’t generalize well; mapping explosion across (model family × seq bucket × SKU × dtype).
- More brittle; cannot express clean fallback behavior beyond ad hoc entries.

## Consequences
### Positive
- Clear, deterministic, auditable selection decisions.
- Faster mitigation via denylist + explicit fallback ordering.
- Easier to support Dedicated/Private customers with constrained GPU SKUs.

### Negative / tradeoffs
- Rules require ongoing maintenance as new model families, kernels, and SKUs are onboarded.
- Performance may lag a learned approach in edge cases; mitigated by expanding benchmark coverage and iterating on rules.

## Acceptance criteria
- No correctness regressions vs v1 across the benchmark matrix.
- Deterministic replay: same (model version, seq bucket, GPU SKU, config version) yields identical selection.
- Bounded overhead: selector evaluation and telemetry sampling do not measurably impact p95 latency (guarded by microbench).
- Documented, tested fallback behavior including “fallback to v1” and denylist hot reload.

## Rollout notes
- v2 ships behind gates; initial canary in Hosted regions, then Dedicated pools.
- Rollback authority: Release Engineering + Runtime oncall per runbook; denylist and model-level disablement are the preferred first actions before full rollback.

## Appendix: example policy table (illustrative)
This example is for documentation only; the source of truth is the runtime kernel registry + policy config.
- If model family = `llama` and seq bucket = `long_ctx` and GPU SKU = `nvidia-l40s-48gb`:
  1) Prefer `kernel_prefill_conservative_v3`
  2) Else fallback to `kernel_prefill_baseline_v2`
  3) Else fallback to v1

- If GPU driver in denylisted range for `kernel_flashdecode_v5`:
  - Block `kernel_flashdecode_v5` and select next eligible candidate.

## Change log
- 2026-02-16: Clarified fallback step 3 (cross-SKU safe baseline requires explicit certification) and added explicit exclusion of utilization-based selection inputs.
- 2026-01-10: Initial acceptance of rule-based approach, feature set, and fallback precedence.

title: ADR-017: Kernel Auto-Selection v2 — Feature Set, Rule-Based Decisioning, and Fallback Policy
preview:
## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind feature gates; rollout in progress for Runtime 1.21).

## Context
Kernel auto-selection v1 relies on a small set of heuristics and a mostly global “best default” per model family. This has led to:
- Tail latency spikes (p95/p99) when long-context or mixed workloads are misclassified.
- Throughput instability across heterogeneous fleets (A100/H100/L40S) where the optimal kernel differs by SKU.
- Slow incident response when a kernel becomes unstable on a specific driver/CUDA combo; v1 lacked a consistent, centrally-managed fallback and denylist policy.

Kernel auto-selection v2 needs to:
- Improve latency and throughput stability with minimal runtime overhead.
- Preserve **correctness** and **determinism** (selection is replayable given the same inputs).
- Support safe rollout and rapid rollback with per-model and per-SKU controls.
- Be explainable and operationally debuggable (oncall can understand why a kernel was chosen and how to override it).

This ADR records (1) the chosen selection approach, (2) the feature set available to the selector, and (3) the explicit fallback policy.

## Decision
### 1) Selection approach: rule-based decisioning (not learned scoring)
We will implement kernel auto-selection v2 as a **deterministic, rule-based decision system** with a small, ordered set of candidate selection rules keyed on:
- Model family / architecture
- Sequence-length regime (prefill vs decode characteristics; context length buckets)
- GPU SKU / capability profile

The selector may compute a simple **rule score / ordering** internally, but it must be interpretable, deterministic, and driven by explicit config + compiled-in rules. We will **not** deploy a learned model (ML-based scoring) for kernel selection in v2.

**Rationale**
- **Explainability and debugging:** Oncall and customers (Dedicated/Private) require a clear mapping from inputs → decision.
- **Rollback safety:** Rule changes can be pinned, gated, and rolled back quickly. Learned scoring introduces opaque regressions and heavier offline evaluation requirements.
- **Determinism guarantees:** A learned system would likely depend on richer/continuous signals and could introduce nondeterminism via feature drift, sampling, or dependency updates.
- **Operational constraints:** Private deployments may not be able to ship frequent model/scoring updates; rules + config can be managed more conservatively.

### 2) Feature set used by v2
The selector’s decision inputs are limited to the following feature categories. Any future features must be added via a new ADR or an amendment to this one.

#### A. Model family / architecture signals (required)
The selector will classify the request into a normalized model family tag based on the loaded model metadata:
- Examples: `llama`, `mistral`, `qwen`, `gemma` (extensible)
- Derived from: model config (architecture name), tokenizer/model repo tags, and Redwood-internal model registry annotations.
- Versioning: model family tag is computed deterministically from model metadata and pinned to the loaded model version.

Notes:
- Variants (e.g., instruct vs base, different rope scaling) may map to the same family but can be further differentiated via optional sub-tags when needed for kernel compatibility.

#### B. Sequence-length regime signals (required)
The selector will bucket each request into a small set of context-length / regime classes:
- `short_ctx`: typical prompts with small prefill (low total input tokens)
- `mid_ctx`
- `long_ctx`: long-context prompts where prefill dominates and tail risks are highest

Additionally, the selector may use a derived “prefill/decode dominance hint” if available (e.g., expected decode length from request `max_tokens` and historic workload hints).

Constraints:
- Buckets must be **stable** (explicit thresholds in config; no adaptive bucketing at request time).
- Bucket boundaries must be tracked and versioned to preserve replayability in incident analysis.

#### C. GPU SKU / capability signals (required)
The selector will use a normalized GPU SKU identifier and a small derived capability profile.
- SKU examples: `nvidia-a100-80gb`, `nvidia-h100-80gb`, `nvidia-l40s-48gb`
- Capability profile may include: `sm_version`, `hbm_gb`, `supports_fp8`, `supports_bf16`, and driver/CUDA compatibility flags.

Normalization requirements:
- Cloud instance naming, MIG variants, and vendor strings must map to a canonical SKU in the runtime.
- MIG instances must be treated as separate capacity profiles if kernel behavior differs materially.

#### D. Optional: scheduler-provided workload hints (allowed, gated)
The scheduler may pass a coarse “workload distribution hint” (e.g., observed seq-length distribution for the pool/route) to stabilize selection for mixed workloads.
- This signal is **optional** and must be behind a feature flag.
- The selector must remain deterministic: for replay, the hint must be logged with the selection event when used.

#### E. Explicitly excluded features (v2)
To reduce nondeterminism and complexity, v2 will not use:
- Live GPU utilization / queue depth as selection inputs (belongs to scheduling/routing).
- Per-request performance feedback loops (online learning).
- Request content features (PII/security concerns; also unstable).

### 3) Fallback policy (missing / unstable kernels)
The selector must always produce a safe kernel choice or fail closed with a clear error classification. Fallback behavior is deterministic and ordered.

#### Definitions
- **Candidate set:** all kernels that are (a) compatible with the model family, (b) compiled/available for the GPU SKU, and (c) not blocked by policy.
- **Policy blocks:** denylist entries at any of the following levels:
  - (kernel, GPU SKU)
  - (kernel, driver/CUDA range)
  - (kernel, model family)
  - (model, GPU SKU) for v2 enablement

#### Fallback precedence (ordered)
When the “preferred” kernel for a (model family, seq bucket, GPU SKU) is not eligible:
1. **Same family, same regime, next-best kernel for that GPU SKU** (as defined by the rule table ordering).
2. **Same family, conservative kernel for that GPU SKU** (known-stable baseline; may trade throughput for predictable tail).
3. **Cross-SKU safe baseline kernel** (portable kernel variant known to be correct across SKUs; only if compatibility is explicitly certified).
4. **Kernel-selection v1 behavior** (only if v2 is enabled but v2 cannot produce an eligible candidate; emits a telemetry event `fallback_to_v1`).
5. **Fail closed** with error `KERNEL_SELECTION_NO_ELIGIBLE_CANDIDATE` if neither v2 nor v1 can safely select a kernel.

Rules:
- Fallbacks 1–3 must not change numerics beyond the established tolerance for that model + dtype configuration.
- Any fallback to v1 or fail-closed is considered a “selection degradation” and is surfaced in rollout dashboards.

#### Unstable kernel handling
If a kernel is observed unstable (crashes, incorrect outputs, or large tail regressions) on a specific SKU/driver combination:
- The immediate mitigation is to add a **denylist entry** at the narrowest safe scope (prefer (kernel, driver/CUDA range, SKU)).
- If needed, also disable v2 for the affected (model, SKU) pair to avoid repeated fallback churn.
- Denylist updates must be hot-reloadable in Hosted and Dedicated control planes; for Private, they are delivered via the approved config update mechanism.

#### Guardrail: driver/CUDA compatibility
If the runtime detects a GPU driver/CUDA version in a known-unstable range for v2 selection (or for specific kernels), v2 must:
- Either preemptively disable the affected kernels via denylist rules, or
- Disable v2 for that environment and fall back to v1.

## Operational implications
### Configuration and controls
v2 must support the following control planes:
- **Feature gate:** `kernel_selection_v2_enabled` at environment/region/pool scope.
- **Allowlist/denylist:** per-model and per-SKU enablement gates.
- **Override:** runtime flag to force a kernel (break-glass), and/or pin kernel choice at route/model scope for customer escalations.

### Telemetry requirements (summary)
Every selection decision must emit a structured event (sampled as needed) that includes:
- Selected kernel ID + version
- Candidate set (or candidate count + top-N IDs, depending on sampling/cardinality limits)
- Model family tag, model version
- Seq bucket and relevant thresholds version
- GPU SKU normalized string + driver/CUDA versions
- Any applied fallback reason codes (e.g., `DENYLISTED_KERNEL`, `MISSING_BINARY`, `UNSUPPORTED_SKU`, `DRIVER_GUARDRAIL`, `FALLBACK_TO_V1`)

## Alternatives considered
### A) Learned scoring model (offline trained)
Pros:
- Potentially captures complex interactions across model/seq/SKU dimensions.

Cons (rejected):
- Harder to explain and debug in incident response.
- Requires a robust offline training + validation pipeline and frequent refresh.
- Increases rollout risk and complicates deterministic replay.

### B) Pure static mapping table (no rules)
Pros:
- Simple implementation.

Cons (rejected):
- Doesn’t generalize well; mapping explosion across (model family × seq bucket × SKU × dtype).
- More brittle; cannot express clean fallback behavior beyond ad hoc entries.

## Consequences
### Positive
- Clear, deterministic, auditable selection decisions.
- Faster mitigation via denylist + explicit fallback ordering.
- Easier to support Dedicated/Private customers with constrained GPU SKUs.

### Negative / tradeoffs
- Rules require ongoing maintenance as new model families, kernels, and SKUs are onboarded.
- Performance may lag a learned approach in edge cases; mitigated by expanding benchmark coverage and iterating on rules.

## Acceptance criteria
- No correctness regressions vs v1 across the benchmark matrix.
- Deterministic replay: same (model version, seq bucket, GPU SKU, config version) yields identical selection.
- Bounded overhead: selector evaluation and telemetry sampling do not measurably impact p95 latency (guarded by microbench).
- Documented, tested fallback behavior including “fallback to v1” and denylist hot reload.

## Rollout notes
- v2 ships behind gates; initial canary in Hosted regions, then Dedicated pools.
- Rollback authority: Release Engineering + Runtime oncall per runbook; denylist and model-level disablement are the preferred first actions before full rollback.

## Appendix: example policy table (illustrative)
This example is for documentation only; the source of truth is the runtime kernel registry + policy config.
- If model family = `llama` and seq bucket = `long_ctx` and GPU SKU = `nvidia-l40s-48gb`:
  1) Prefer `kernel_prefill_conservative_v3`
  2) Else fallback to `kernel_prefill_baseline_v2`
  3) Else fallback to v1

- If GPU driver in denylisted range for `kernel_flashdecode_v5`:
  - Block `kernel_flashdecode_v5` and select next eligible candidate.

## Change log
- 2026-02-16: Clarified fallback step 3 (cross-SKU safe baseline requires explicit certification) and added explicit exclusion of utilization-based selection inputs.
- 2026-01-10: Initial acceptance of rule-based approach, feature set, and fallback precedence.
