- 2026-02-12: Added eviction selector health metrics and standardized fragmentation_ratio definition based on largest contiguous free span.
- 2026-01-12: Initial publication for runtime 1.22 compaction rollout.

source.title: Config Consistency and Gated Feature Flags Playbook
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Overview:
This playbook defines our standard architecture and operational practices for distributing runtime configuration (feature flags, quantization/precision profiles, autoscaling hints, and routing policies) with a focus on strong eventual consistency, safe progressive rollouts, and automated SLI/SLO safety gates. It is intended for platform engineers, runtime teams, and service owners who operate multi-tenant inference services (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

Problem statement:
- Runtime configuration changes (flags, model variants, quantization profiles) are frequent and can change behavior across thousands of nodes instantly.
- Inconsistencies between control-plane config and node application config cause correctness, performance regressions, and hard-to-debug incidents.
- Feature-flag-driven rollouts (canary, ring, percentage rollouts) must be reversible with bounded blast radius and observable safety checks.

Scope:
- Applies to: feature flags, model variant assignments, quantization/precision toggles, cache-affinity rules, routing hints, and throttling profiles.
- Not in scope: long-lived static infra config (provisioning manifests), secret material (handled by KMS/HSM policies).

Definitions:
- Source of truth (SoT): primary control-plane config store (Redwood Config Service).
- Node view: local cached copy of the config used by a runtime instance.
- Rollout policy: a declarative object describing targeting rules, phases, and safety gates.
- Safety gate: an automated check (SLI/SLO, error delta, latency delta) that can pause/rollback a rollout phase.

Principles (high level):
1) Single authoritative SoT with versioned config artifacts.
2) Compare-and-apply semantics: nodes only transition state when they can validate the new artifact version and dependencies.
3) Observable rollouts: every change creates a rollout event that is traced, auditable, and linked to telemetry.
4) Fail-safe by default: nodes must reject partial or incompatible config and fall back to the previous compatible version.
5) Minimize coupling: config changes should be small, well-scoped, and backward-compatible where possible.

Architecture patterns (recommended):
- Control plane: Redwood Config Service (RCS) publishes versioned manifests and rollout policies; maintains a change log (immutable) and provides delta queries.
- Distribution: hybrid push/pull model. RCS pushes notifications; nodes pull deltas over gRPC with integrity checks.
- Local store: each node persists last-known-good artifact in a local fast store (lmdb or similar) with a strict atomic swap.
- Dependency graph: manifests include explicit dependency checks (e.g., model-variant vX requires runtime 3.4+).

Compatibility table (summary):
| Artifact Type | Min Runtime | Validation | Fallback Strategy |
|---------------|-------------|------------|-------------------|
| Quantization profile | 3.3 | dry-run on staging slice, mismatch -> reject | keep previous profile |
| Feature flag rules | any | schema + histogram sanity check | disable feature for tenant |
| Routing hint set | 3.5 | sample routing simulation | route via default path |

Distribution mechanics (detailed):
- Notification layer: RCS emits a signed change notice to a pub/sub topic (regioned).
- Node reaction: subscribing nodes receive a notice and then call RCS DeltaFetch(running_version, candidate_version) over TLS.
- DeltaFetch returns: compressed diff, signature, dependency manifest, and a preflight validation plan (optional).
- Atomic install: nodes write the candidate to local store and run the preflight validations. Only on success do they atomically swap the active pointer "current_artifact".
- Heartbeat: after swap, node reports a rollout event with: artifact_version, node_id, tenant_id (if applicable), preflight results, and short telemetry sample.

Rollout policy primitives (json-like reference):
- targets: [tenant-id or global]
- phases: [{pct: 1, duration: 10m}, {pct: 10, duration: 1h}, {pct: 100, duration: 6h}]
- safety_gates: ["slo_error_rate_delta < 0.5%", "latency_p50_delta < 10ms"]
- rollback_on: ["error_rate_increase > 1%", "model_quality_regression > 3%"]

Progressive rollout procedure (operational steps):
1) Prepare: author creates versioned artifact and a rollout policy in RCS. Include explicit test tenants and staging slices.
2) Dry-run: enable the artifact for a staging slice (0.5–1% traffic) with logging and observability level elevated.
3) Observability verification (must-pass):
   - error rate delta across staging slice vs baseline < configured threshold
   - latency p50/p95 regressions under configured limits
   - unit/e2e quality metrics (where applicable) show no regression
4) Phase advance: after automated gates pass, advance to next phase. Manual hold allowed for non-automatic rollouts.
5) Pause and rollback triggers: any gate failing triggers automated rollback to previous version and creates an incident with tags rollout:auto-rollback.
6) Post-rollout: keep the artifact in SoT history and run a 72-hour postmortem checks for any delayed regressions.

Safety gates and SLI mapping (table):
| Safety Gate Name | Measured SLI | Threshold | Reaction | Owner |
|------------------|--------------|-----------|----------|-------|
| ErrorRateDelta | service.error_rate | > 0.75% absolute increase | auto-rollback | runtime-oncall |
| LatencyTail | service.latency_p95 | > 20% relative increase | pause rollout, alert | runtime-oncall |
| ModelDrift | model.eval.delta_f1 | > 2% absolute drop | pause + rollback candidate | applied-ml |

Validation and test strategies:
- Shadow testing: run candidate logic in shadow mode for a percentage of production traffic. Aggregate telemetry but do not affect responses.
- Replay testing: replay sampled production traffic against candidate in isolated canary cluster prior to any production rollout.
- A/B measurement: use bucketed clients or tenant-level bucketing to gather statistically significant differences.

Failure modes and mitigations (selected):
1) Partial install due to disk corruption -> mitigation: local store checksum + reject candidate.
2) Incompatible dependency (runtime mismatch) -> mitigation: block install and emit dependency failure event; optionally offer a compat shim for simple cases.
3) Pub/sub notification flapping -> mitigation: RCS implements a debounce window and minimum-notify interval; nodes dedupe by artifact id.

Runbook: node-level incident (short):
1) Detect: alert fires for rollout.auto-rollback or safety gate violation.
2) Contain: RCS automatically halts the rollout; shift traffic away from affected tenants if identified.
3) Triage: runtime-oncall collects rollout_event, node logs, preflight results, and telemetry windows.
4) Remediate: revert artifact in RCS, push blocking patch if necessary, or enable emergency safe-mode.
5) Post-incident: open postmortem; tag with rollout id, artifact version, and affected tenant list.

Ownership and responsibilities:
- RCS team: control plane, signature keys, rollout policy UI/CLI, audit log retention (90 days).
- Runtime team: implement pull/delta protocol, atomic swap, local store durability, and safety gate enforcement.
- Applied ML: provide model quality checks and preflight evaluators.
- Observability: own SLI definitions, alerting thresholds, and rollout dashboards.

Rollout checklist (pre-launch):
- [ ] Artifact schema validated and signed.
- [ ] Dependency matrix verified.
- [ ] Staging shadow run completed for >= 1h.
- [ ] Telemetry hooks present and dashboards ready.
- [ ] Rollout policy reviewed and approved by runtime and applied-ml owners.

CLI example (reference):
# create artifact and rollout
redwood-config create-artifact --file quant-profile-v3.yaml --sign --meta '{"author":"ava.chen"}'
redwood-config start-rollout --artifact quant-profile-v3 --policy quick-canary.json

YAML snippet (artifact manifest sample):
artifact_id: quant-profile-v3
min_runtime: 3.3.1
checksum: sha256:abcd1234...
dependencies:
  - name: attention-kernel
    min_version: 1.7.0
preflight:
  - type: shadow-sample
    sample_pct: 1.0

Telemetry and dashboards (must-haves):
- Rollout heatmap (phases vs. clusters)
- Per-tenant error rate and latency delta panels with baseline comparisons
- Rollout timeline linked to traces and change log entries

Related pages:
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/adaptive-kv-coordinator-and-eviction-contract-2026
- /confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/telemetry-contract-for-ml-inference-features-2027

Appendix: quick decision record (why hybrid push/pull):
- Push-only risks: flooding and difficulty with integrity verification at scale.
- Pull-only risks: slower reaction times and additional control-plane load at rollout spikes.
- Hybrid gives timely notification with robust integrity and allows nodes to throttle/pull deltas when under pressure.

Document history:
- 2025-09-18 initial draft (Ava Chen)
- 2025-11-02 added safety gate table and CLI examples (Rafael Ortiz review)
- 2026-02-12 formalized runbook and rollout checklist (Priya Nair review)

Contact: runtime-systems@redwood.internal (primary), rcs-team@redwood.internal (control plane)

title: Config Consistency and Gated Feature Flags Playbook
preview:
Overview:
This playbook defines our standard architecture and operational practices for distributing runtime configuration (feature flags, quantization/precision profiles, autoscaling hints, and routing policies) with a focus on strong eventual consistency, safe progressive rollouts, and automated SLI/SLO safety gates. It is intended for platform engineers, runtime teams, and service owners who operate multi-tenant inference services (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

Problem statement:
- Runtime configuration changes (flags, model variants, quantization profiles) are frequent and can change behavior across thousands of nodes instantly.
- Inconsistencies between control-plane config and node application config cause correctness, performance regressions, and hard-to-debug incidents.
- Feature-flag-driven rollouts (canary, ring, percentage rollouts) must be reversible with bounded blast radius and observable safety checks.

Scope:
- Applies to: feature flags, model variant assignments, quantization/precision toggles, cache-affinity rules, routing hints, and throttling profiles.
- Not in scope: long-lived static infra config (provisioning manifests), secret material (handled by KMS/HSM policies).

Definitions:
- Source of truth (SoT): primary control-plane config store (Redwood Config Service).
- Node view: local cached copy of the config used by a runtime instance.
- Rollout policy: a declarative object describing targeting rules, phases, and safety gates.
- Safety gate: an automated check (SLI/SLO, error delta, latency delta) that can pause/rollback a rollout phase.

Principles (high level):
1) Single authoritative SoT with versioned config artifacts.
2) Compare-and-apply semantics: nodes only transition state when they can validate the new artifact version and dependencies.
3) Observable rollouts: every change creates a rollout event that is traced, auditable, and linked to telemetry.
4) Fail-safe by default: nodes must reject partial or incompatible config and fall back to the previous compatible version.
5) Minimize coupling: config changes should be small, well-scoped, and backward-compatible where possible.

Architecture patterns (recommended):
- Control plane: Redwood Config Service (RCS) publishes versioned manifests and rollout policies; maintains a change log (immutable) and provides delta queries.
- Distribution: hybrid push/pull model. RCS pushes notifications; nodes pull deltas over gRPC with integrity checks.
- Local store: each node persists last-known-good artifact in a local fast store (lmdb or similar) with a strict atomic swap.
- Dependency graph: manifests include explicit dependency checks (e.g., model-variant vX requires runtime 3.4+).

Compatibility table (summary):
| Artifact Type | Min Runtime | Validation | Fallback Strategy |
|---------------|-------------|------------|-------------------|
| Quantization profile | 3.3 | dry-run on staging slice, mismatch -> reject | keep previous profile |
| Feature flag rules | any | schema + histogram sanity check | disable feature for tenant |
| Routing hint set | 3.5 | sample routing simulation | route via default path |

Distribution mechanics (detailed):
- Notification layer: RCS emits a signed change notice to a pub/sub topic (regioned).
- Node reaction: subscribing nodes receive a notice and then call RCS DeltaFetch(running_version, candidate_version) over TLS.
- DeltaFetch returns: compressed diff, signature, dependency manifest, and a preflight validation plan (optional).
- Atomic install: nodes write the candidate to local store and run the preflight validations. Only on success do they atomically swap the active pointer \"current_artifact\".
- Heartbeat: after swap, node reports a rollout event with: artifact_version, node_id, tenant_id (if applicable), preflight results, and short telemetry sample.

Rollout policy primitives (json-like reference):
- targets: [tenant-id or global]
- phases: [{pct: 1, duration: 10m}, {pct: 10, duration: 1h}, {pct: 100, duration: 6h}]
- safety_gates: [\"slo_error_rate_delta < 0.5%\", \"latency_p50_delta < 10ms\"]
- rollback_on: [\"error_rate_increase > 1%\", \"model_quality_regression > 3%\"]

Progressive rollout procedure (operational steps):
1) Prepare: author creates versioned artifact and a rollout policy in RCS. Include explicit test tenants and staging slices.
2) Dry-run: enable the artifact for a staging slice (0.5–1% traffic) with logging and observability level elevated.
3) Observability verification (must-pass):
   - error rate delta across staging slice vs baseline < configured threshold
   - latency p50/p95 regressions under configured limits
   - unit/e2e quality metrics (where applicable) show no regression
4) Phase advance: after automated gates pass, advance to next phase. Manual hold allowed for non-automatic rollouts.
5) Pause and rollback triggers: any gate failing triggers automated rollback to previous version and creates an incident with tags rollout:auto-rollback.
6) Post-rollout: keep the artifact in SoT history and run a 72-hour postmortem checks for any delayed regressions.

Safety gates and SLI mapping (table):
| Safety Gate Name | Measured SLI | Threshold | Reaction | Owner |
|------------------|--------------|-----------|----------|-------|
| ErrorRateDelta | service.error_rate | > 0.75% absolute increase | auto-rollback | runtime-oncall |
| LatencyTail | service.latency_p95 | > 20% relative increase | pause rollout, alert | runtime-oncall |
| ModelDrift | model.eval.delta_f1 | > 2% absolute drop | pause + rollback candidate | applied-ml |

Validation and test strategies:
- Shadow testing: run candidate logic in shadow mode for a percentage of production traffic. Aggregate telemetry but do not affect responses.
- Replay testing: replay sampled production traffic against candidate in isolated canary cluster prior to any production rollout.
- A/B measurement: use bucketed clients or tenant-level bucketing to gather statistically significant differences.

Failure modes and mitigations (selected):
1) Partial install due to disk corruption -> mitigation: local store checksum + reject candidate.
2) Incompatible dependency (runtime mismatch) -> mitigation: block install and emit dependency failure event; optionally offer a compat shim for simple cases.
3) Pub/sub notification flapping -> mitigation: RCS implements a debounce window and minimum-notify interval; nodes dedupe by artifact id.

Runbook: node-level incident (short):
1) Detect: alert fires for rollout.auto-rollback or safety gate violation.
2) Contain: RCS automatically halts the rollout; shift traffic away from affected tenants if identified.
3) Triage: runtime-oncall collects rollout_event, node logs, preflight results, and telemetry windows.
4) Remediate: revert artifact in RCS, push blocking patch if necessary, or enable emergency safe-mode.
5) Post-incident: open postmortem; tag with rollout id, artifact version, and affected tenant list.

Ownership and responsibilities:
- RCS team: control plane, signature keys, rollout policy UI/CLI, audit log retention (90 days).
- Runtime team: implement pull/delta protocol, atomic swap, local store durability, and safety gate enforcement.
- Applied ML: provide model quality checks and preflight evaluators.
- Observability: own SLI definitions, alerting thresholds, and rollout dashboards.

Rollout checklist (pre-launch):
- [ ] Artifact schema validated and signed.
- [ ] Dependency matrix verified.
- [ ] Staging shadow run completed for >= 1h.
- [ ] Telemetry hooks present and dashboards ready.
- [ ] Rollout policy reviewed and approved by runtime and applied-ml owners.

CLI example (reference):
# create artifact and rollout
redwood-config create-artifact --file quant-profile-v3.yaml --sign --meta '{\"author\":\"ava.chen\"}'
redwood-config start-rollout --artifact quant-profile-v3 --policy quick-canary.json

YAML snippet (artifact manifest sample):
artifact_id: quant-profile-v3
min_runtime: 3.3.1
checksum: sha256:abcd1234...
dependencies:
  - name: attention-kernel
    min_version: 1.7.0
preflight:
  - type: shadow-sample
    sample_pct: 1.0

Telemetry and dashboards (must-haves):
- Rollout heatmap (phases vs. clusters)
- Per-tenant error rate and latency delta panels with baseline comparisons
- Rollout timeline linked to traces and change log entries

Related pages:
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/adaptive-kv-coordinator-and-eviction-contract-2026
- /confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/telemetry-contract-for-ml-inference-features-2027

Appendix: quick decision record (why hybrid push/pull):
- Push-only risks: flooding and difficulty with integrity verification at scale.
- Pull-only risks: slower reaction times and additional control-plane load at rollout spikes.
- Hybrid gives timely notification with robust integrity and allows nodes to throttle/pull deltas when under pressure.

Document history:
- 2025-09-18 initial draft (Ava Chen)
- 2025-11-02 added safety gate table and CLI examples (Rafael Ortiz review)
- 2026-02-12 formalized runbook and rollout checklist (Priya Nair review)

Contact: runtime-systems@redwood.internal (primary), rcs-team@redwood.internal (control plane)

source.title: Service surface minimization and compatibility playbook
source.author: Aisha K. Patel
source.content:
Overview

Purpose:
This playbook captures Redwood's recommended patterns for minimizing API/service surface area and evolving request/response schemas with minimal consumer disruption. It consolidates practical rules, examples, testing strategies, and rollout procedures so teams can safely ship changes that maintain production stability and predictable client cost/latency profiles.

Scope:
Applies to all externally-facing Redwood APIs (hosted and private control plane) and internal service-to-service contracts that impact inference, telemetry, billing, or routing decisions. It complements the API compatibility & deprecation policy but focuses on design patterns and operational controls.

Definitions:
- Service surface: set of public endpoints, RPC methods, query parameters, header hooks, and documented request/response fields a consumer depends on.
- Contract: the machine- and human-readable schema, including metadata, error models, and behavior guarantees (idempotency, ordering, streaming semantics).
- Consumer-driven contract (CDC): tests or assertions derived from actual client usage to validate compatibility.

Principles (high level):
1. Minimize: Only expose fields and endpoints that deliver measurable consumer value. Prefer extensible generic fields over many specialized endpoints.
2. Versionless-first: Default to non-major breaking changes. Reserve explicit versioning for architectural shifts.
3. Strict rollout controls: All changes that touch contracts require canarying, CDCs, automated regression checks, and a documented fallback.
4. Observable compatibility: Surface compatibility impact in dashboards (consumer break rate, schema-change alerting).
5. Fail-open consumer safety: Keep benign defaults; prefer ignoring unknown optional fields over rejecting requests.

Standards and patterns

A. Service-surface minimization checklist (apply during design)
- Does the change add a new endpoint or can we extend an existing one? If possible, prefer extension.
- Can we reuse existing metadata headers instead of adding new header keys?
- Is the new field optional with a safe default? If yes, make it optional.
- Will the change increase cardinality of route variations (query params + headers)? Aim to keep cardinality low.
- Is there a measurable feature flag or telemetry guard to limit rollout?

B. Field and schema rules
1. Additive-only policy for minor releases:
   - Adding new optional fields to response/request is allowed. New fields MUST be optional and documented.
   - Removing fields or changing types is a breaking change and requires ADR + major-versioning or migration plan.
2. Stringly-typed extensibility pattern:
   - For rapidly-evolving bits, prefer a single extensible map field ("attributes" or "metadata") with limited keyset enforcement server-side.
3. Enumerations and feature flags:
   - New enum variants are allowed if consumers ignore unknown enum values gracefully. Document opt-in rollout for client-side consumers.
4. Error model stability:
   - Error codes must be stable strings (e.g., RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND). Avoid encoding semantics in error text.

C. Endpoint minimization patterns
- Composite endpoints: Where multiple small endpoints exist (e.g., /model/summary, /model/metrics), provide a single /model?fields=summary,metrics style endpoint to reduce route surface.
- Projection query param: Support ?fields= and ?exclude= to return minimal payloads; default should be compact (no heavy embeddings or long metadata).
- Streaming separation: Separate streaming and non-streaming paths (e.g., /generate vs /generate/stream) to avoid extra complexity in the base contract.

D. Compatibility opt-in controls
- Feature gates at the user-token level for new schema fields.
- Compatibility headers: X-Redwood-Client-Capabilities (comma-separated tokens) to allow servers to detect client features without expanding route variants.

Examples

1) Safe additive response (recommended)
Request:
```http
GET /v1/models/coffee-shop HTTP/1.1
Host: api.redwood.ai
Authorization: Bearer <token>
```
Response (existing):
```json
{
  "id": "coffee-shop",
  "name": "Coffee Shop Model",
  "description": "small text model for conversational tasks"
}
```
Add new optional field (safe):
```json
{
  "id": "coffee-shop",
  "name": "Coffee Shop Model",
  "description": "small text model for conversational tasks",
  "serving_profiles": ["low-latency", "balanced"] // optional array, safe to ignore
}
```

2) Avoid changing semantics of existing fields (anti-pattern)
- Do NOT change numeric field units (tokens -> characters) or flip boolean polarity ("enabled" -> meaning reversed). Such changes require explicit migration.

Schema-evolution workflows

1. Proposal and schema PR:
- Create an API design doc describing intent, consumer impact, and telemetry. Attach example requests/responses.
- Add schema diff in the PR and a migration plan if the change is non-additive.

2. Automated compatibility checks (required):
- Run CDC test suite (see Testing section) and contract linter. Changes that fail must be gated.
- Run production traffic replay against canary to check deserialization errors and parity metrics.

3. Canary rollout (required for any schema touch)
- Canary on 0.5–2% of traffic for 48 hours. Monitor: deserialization errors, 4xx spike, consumer error rate, latency P95.
- If any of these breach configured thresholds (see Monitoring section), auto-rollback.

4. Gradual ramp and deprecation
- If removal is required, mark field deprecated in docs, run consumer impact analysis, and keep deprecated field for at least two major release cycles (target 90 days minimum).

Testing and automation

Required tests before merge:
- Unit schema validation (JSON Schema / Protobuf linter).
- Consumer-Driven Contract tests: Each consumer team must maintain a minimal CDC verifying their expected contract slices.
- Mutation testing: Validate server tolerates unknown optional fields and clients tolerate unknown response fields.
- Integration replay: Use a recorded traffic snippet to exercise new code-paths.

CI automation checklist (example):
1. schema-lint (fail on breaking diffs unless ADR referenced)
2. cdc-run (execute CDC tests against a mock server)
3. replay-smoke (run traffic replay job)
4. canary-deploy job (automatically wires canary config)

Monitoring and thresholds

Create a per-change dashboard with the following tiles:
- Deserialization error rate (target < 0.01% increase)
- Client 4xx rate delta (target < +0.2% absolute)
- Client error spike count (>= 5 spike events in 15m triggers pager)
- Latency P95/P99 delta (target < +10% relative)
- Consumer failure-rate by client-id (ranked)

Automated rollback triggers (examples):
- Deserialization error rate > 0.05% sustained for 5 minutes
- Any single client ID sees >1% absolute increase in 5xx or deserialization errors
- Latency P99 increases by >25% with sustained SLA breach

Rollout and rollback playbook

1. Pre-rollout
- Ensure automated CDCs pass.
- Prepare rollback plan: Kubernetes manifest or feature flag reversal command and owner.
- Schedule a 2-hour watch window after canary start.

2. Canary
- Deploy to canary pool. Start collecting metrics.
- After 2 hours with no threshold breaches, increment traffic to 10% for 24 hours.

3. Ramp to GA
- Ramp in exponential steps (10% -> 30% -> 60% -> 100%) with at least 24 hours at each step for non-trivial schema changes.

4. Rollback
- If trigger fires, invoke rollback runbook: revert canary, disable feature flag, notify owners, open a postmortem within 72 hours.

Ownership and governance

- API owner: the owning service team must maintain schema docs, CDCs, and rollout artifact.
- Platform guardrail: Platform team provides schema-lint, CDC harness, replay tooling, and the canary deployment pipeline.
- Change advisory: For changes affecting payment, routing, or privacy-sensitive fields, involve product and security reviewers.

Appendix: quick reference

Table: Allowed vs. Breaking changes
| Change type | Allowed in patch/minor | Requires ADR/major | Notes |
|------------|-----------------------:|------------------:|-------|
| Add optional response field | Yes | No | Preferred pattern |
| Add required request field | No | Yes | Use feature gate first |
| Change field type or unit | No | Yes | Migration required |
| Remove field | No | Yes | Deprecate first |
| Add endpoint | Generally yes | No | Prefer extension instead |

Contacts and references:
- Platform: eng-platform@redwood.ai (canary pipeline, schema-lint)
- Contract testing docs: sources/confluence/eng-developer-experience/api-examples/contract-testing-harness-2025.json
- Deprecation policy: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/api-standards/api-compatibility-and-deprecation-policy-2026.json

Change log:
- 2025-11-03: Initial draft by Aisha K. Patel.
- 2025-12-10: Added CDC requirements and canary thresholds (reviewed by Daniel M. Cho).
- 2026-02-12: Clarified monitoring thresholds and rollback triggers (MaraDa Gf3nzalez reviewed).

title: Service surface minimization and compatibility playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose:
This playbook captures Redwood's recommended patterns for minimizing API/service surface area and evolving request/response schemas with minimal consumer disruption. It consolidates practical rules, examples, testing strategies, and rollout procedures so teams can safely ship changes that maintain production stability and predictable client cost/latency profiles.

Scope:
Applies to all externally-facing Redwood APIs (hosted and private control plane) and internal service-to-service contracts that impact inference, telemetry, billing, or routing decisions. It complements the API compatibility & deprecation policy but focuses on design patterns and operational controls.

Definitions:
- Service surface: set of public endpoints, RPC methods, query parameters, header hooks, and documented request/response fields a consumer depends on.
- Contract: the machine- and human-readable schema, including metadata, error models, and behavior guarantees (idempotency, ordering, streaming semantics).
- Consumer-driven contract (CDC): tests or assertions derived from actual client usage to validate compatibility.

Principles (high level):
1. Minimize: Only expose fields and endpoints that deliver measurable consumer value. Prefer extensible generic fields over many specialized endpoints.
2. Versionless-first: Default to non-major breaking changes. Reserve explicit versioning for architectural shifts.
3. Strict rollout controls: All changes that touch contracts require canarying, CDCs, automated regression checks, and a documented fallback.
4. Observable compatibility: Surface compatibility impact in dashboards (consumer break rate, schema-change alerting).
5. Fail-open consumer safety: Keep benign defaults; prefer ignoring unknown optional fields over rejecting requests.

Standards and patterns

A. Service-surface minimization checklist (apply during design)
- Does the change add a new endpoint or can we extend an existing one? If possible, prefer extension.
- Can we reuse existing metadata headers instead of adding new header keys?
- Is the new field optional with a safe default? If yes, make it optional.
- Will the change increase cardinality of route variations (query params + headers)? Aim to keep cardinality low.
- Is there a measurable feature flag or telemetry guard to limit rollout?

B. Field and schema rules
1. Additive-only policy for minor releases:
   - Adding new optional fields to response/request is allowed. New fields MUST be optional and documented.
   - Removing fields or changing types is a breaking change and requires ADR + major-versioning or migration plan.
2. Stringly-typed extensibility pattern:
   - For rapidly-evolving bits, prefer a single extensible map field ("attributes" or "metadata") with limited keyset enforcement server-side.
3. Enumerations and feature flags:
   - New enum variants are allowed if consumers ignore unknown enum values gracefully. Document opt-in rollout for client-side consumers.
4. Error model stability:
   - Error codes must be stable strings (e.g., RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND). Avoid encoding semantics in error text.

C. Endpoint minimization patterns
- Composite endpoints: Where multiple small endpoints exist (e.g., /model/summary, /model/metrics), provide a single /model?fields=summary,metrics style endpoint to reduce route surface.
- Projection query param: Support ?fields= and ?exclude= to return minimal payloads; default should be compact (no heavy embeddings or long metadata).
- Streaming separation: Separate streaming and non-streaming paths (e.g., /generate vs /generate/stream) to avoid extra complexity in the base contract.

D. Compatibility opt-in controls
- Feature gates at the user-token level for new schema fields.
- Compatibility headers: X-Redwood-Client-Capabilities (comma-separated tokens) to allow servers to detect client features without expanding route variants.

Examples

1) Safe additive response (recommended)
Request:
```http
GET /v1/models/coffee-shop HTTP/1.1
Host: api.redwood.ai
Authorization: Bearer <token>
```
Response (existing):
```json
{
  "id": "coffee-shop",
  "name": "Coffee Shop Model",
  "description": "small text model for conversational tasks"
}
```
Add new optional field (safe):
```json
{
  "id": "coffee-shop",
  "name": "Coffee Shop Model",
  "description": "small text model for conversational tasks",
  "serving_profiles": ["low-latency", "balanced"] // optional array, safe to ignore
}
```

2) Avoid changing semantics of existing fields (anti-pattern)
- Do NOT change numeric field units (tokens -> characters) or flip boolean polarity ("enabled" -> meaning reversed). Such changes require explicit migration.

Schema-evolution workflows

1. Proposal and schema PR:
- Create an API design doc describing intent, consumer impact, and telemetry. Attach example requests/responses.
- Add schema diff in the PR and a migration plan if the change is non-additive.

2. Automated compatibility checks (required):
- Run CDC test suite (see Testing section) and contract linter. Changes that fail must be gated.
- Run production traffic replay against canary to check deserialization errors and parity metrics.

3. Canary rollout (required for any schema touch)
- Canary on 0.5–2% of traffic for 48 hours. Monitor: deserialization errors, 4xx spike, consumer error rate, latency P95.
- If any of these breach configured thresholds (see Monitoring section), auto-rollback.

4. Gradual ramp and deprecation
- If removal is required, mark field deprecated in docs, run consumer impact analysis, and keep deprecated field for at least two major release cycles (target 90 days minimum).

Testing and automation

Required tests before merge:
- Unit schema validation (JSON Schema / Protobuf linter).
- Consumer-Driven Contract tests: Each consumer team must maintain a minimal CDC verifying their expected contract slices.
- Mutation testing: Validate server tolerates unknown optional fields and clients tolerate unknown response fields.
- Integration replay: Use a recorded traffic snippet to exercise new code-paths.

CI automation checklist (example):
1. schema-lint (fail on breaking diffs unless ADR referenced)
2. cdc-run (execute CDC tests against a mock server)
3. replay-smoke (run traffic replay job)
4. canary-deploy job (automatically wires canary config)

Monitoring and thresholds

Create a per-change dashboard with the following tiles:
- Deserialization error rate (target < 0.01% increase)
- Client 4xx rate delta (target < +0.2% absolute)
- Client error spike count (>= 5 spike events in 15m triggers pager)
- Latency P95/P99 delta (target < +10% relative)
- Consumer failure-rate by client-id (ranked)

Automated rollback triggers (examples):
- Deserialization error rate > 0.05% sustained for 5 minutes
- Any single client ID sees >1% absolute increase in 5xx or deserialization errors
- Latency P99 increases by >25% with sustained SLA breach

Rollout and rollback playbook

1. Pre-rollout
- Ensure automated CDCs pass.
- Prepare rollback plan: Kubernetes manifest or feature flag reversal command and owner.
- Schedule a 2-hour watch window after canary start.

2. Canary
- Deploy to canary pool. Start collecting metrics.
- After 2 hours with no threshold breaches, increment traffic to 10% for 24 hours.

3. Ramp to GA
- Ramp in exponential steps (10% -> 30% -> 60% -> 100%) with at least 24 hours at each step for non-trivial schema changes.

4. Rollback
- If trigger fires, invoke rollback runbook: revert canary, disable feature flag, notify owners, open a postmortem within 72 hours.

Ownership and governance

- API owner: the owning service team must maintain schema docs, CDCs, and rollout artifact.
- Platform guardrail: Platform team provides schema-lint, CDC harness, replay tooling, and the canary deployment pipeline.
- Change advisory: For changes affecting payment, routing, or privacy-sensitive fields, involve product and security reviewers.

Appendix: quick reference

Table: Allowed vs. Breaking changes
| Change type | Allowed in patch/minor | Requires ADR/major | Notes |
|------------|-----------------------:|------------------:|-------|
| Add optional response field | Yes | No | Preferred pattern |
| Add required request field | No | Yes | Use feature gate first |
| Change field type or unit | No | Yes | Migration required |
| Remove field | No | Yes | Deprecate first |
| Add endpoint | Generally yes | No | Prefer extension instead |

Contacts and references:
- Platform: eng-platform@redwood.ai (canary pipeline, schema-lint)
- Contract testing docs: sources/confluence/eng-developer-experience/api-examples/contract-testing-harness-2025.json
- Deprecation policy: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/api-standards/api-compatibility-and-deprecation-policy-2026.json

Change log:
- 2025-11-03: Initial draft by Aisha K. Patel.
- 2025-12-10: Added CDC requirements and canary thresholds (reviewed by Daniel M. Cho).
- 2026-02-12: Clarified monitoring thresholds and rollback triggers (MaraDa Gf3nzalez reviewed).

source.title: ADR-0142: Perf Canary Baselines, Regression Thresholds, Min Sample Sizes, and Anti-Flap Logic
source.author: Nadia Rahman
source.body:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
This ADR defines how the production performance canary computes baselines and evaluates regressions for per-model serving performance (primarily p95 latency and tokens/sec). It documents:
- Baseline computation approach (rolling vs fixed) and when each is used.
- Regression thresholds (warning vs paging) and how they are applied.
- Minimum sample sizes and confidence requirements to avoid noisy alerts.
- Anti-flap / dedupe logic to prevent alert storms during drift, telemetry delays, or small-sample variance.

## Context / Problem
Redwood’s serving runtime changes frequently (kernel selection updates, scheduler heuristics, model config changes, GPU fleet changes, and staged rollouts). We need an always-on canary that:
- Detects real p95 latency regressions and throughput drops early.
- Produces actionable signals for attribution (kernel vs scheduler vs infra).
- Avoids paging on expected variance and baseline drift (e.g., slow fleet mix changes or regional differences).

The key design tension is baseline choice:
- A **fixed baseline** is stable and easy to reason about during rollouts, but can become stale as hardware/driver stacks and workloads evolve.
- A **rolling baseline** adapts over time, but can drift (masking regressions) or create false positives if drift is misinterpreted as regression.

## Decision Drivers
1. **Low noise / high trust**: warnings should be useful; pages should be rare and high-confidence.
2. **Fast detection**: detect regressions within ~10–30 minutes for top models in covered regions.
3. **Attribution**: baseline keys must align to dimensions needed for root cause (runtime build, kernel tags, SKU).
4. **Operational simplicity**: SRE/on-call should have a predictable interpretation of “regression vs baseline,” including during ongoing rollouts.
5. **Guardrails against drift and telemetry issues**: avoid alert storms caused by baseline drift, delayed metric ingestion, and label cardinality errors.

## Decision
We adopt a **hybrid baseline strategy**:

1) **Primary baseline = Fixed, versioned baseline per (model, model_version, runtime_build, kernel_set_id, hardware_sku) with optional region override**
- Baselines are generated from controlled canary runs (prod-like load) and stored as immutable artifacts.
- Baselines are explicitly tied to the runtime build and kernel selection identifiers so comparisons remain meaningful.
- This is the baseline used for rollout gating and for paging.

2) **Secondary baseline = Rolling “health baseline” used for drift monitoring and threshold calibration (not for paging)**
- We compute a rolling reference from recent stable periods (default: last 7 days) to detect slow environmental drift and to inform recalibration.
- Rolling baseline is not used to auto-suppress a fixed-baseline regression; it is used as a triage and tuning signal.

3) **Baseline freeze rules**
- During a runtime rollout (canary/gradual), the baseline used for alerts is **frozen** to the most recent fixed baseline for the currently active runtime build on that ring.
- If an incident is declared for a perf regression, baseline updates are **paused** for affected keys until incident closure.

4) **Threshold evaluation requires minimum sample size + sustained breach + hysteresis**
- Alerts require both a minimum number of canary requests and sustained breach across multiple evaluation windows.
- Alert clear conditions are stricter than trigger conditions (hysteresis) to reduce flapping.

## Baseline Keys (What defines “like-for-like”)
Baseline artifacts and regression evaluation are keyed by the following dimensions:

**Required baseline key fields (v1):**
- model_id (canonical model identifier)
- model_version (registry version / hash)
- runtime_build_id (serving runtime build tag)
- kernel_set_id (kernel selection/version identifier)
- hardware_sku (e.g., H100-80GB, A100-80GB)

**Optional fields (only when variance requires it):**
- region (enabled for regions with materially different fleet/network characteristics)
- capacity_tier (hosted vs dedicated pools) when pools differ materially

Notes:
- We do NOT key baselines by customer, endpoint, or prompt content (privacy + cardinality constraints).
- Scheduler configuration flags that materially change behavior must be reflected either in runtime_build_id or an explicit scheduler_profile tag (introduced only if needed; kept off by default to control cardinality).

## Metrics Covered
Baselines and regressions are computed for:
- End-to-end latency: p50, p95, p99 (ms)
- Runtime-only latency: p50, p95, p99 (ms) (excludes network/client)
- Prefill latency and decode latency (ms)
- Queueing time (ms) (scheduler-level)
- Tokens/sec (overall, and decode tokens/sec where available)
- Batch size distribution (for attribution)
- KV cache hit rate (where applicable; informational at v1)

## Baseline Computation
### Fixed baseline artifact
- Source: controlled perf-canary runs using the approved synthetic prompt distribution.
- For each baseline key, we store:
  - distribution summaries (p50/p95/p99) for each latency metric
  - mean and standard deviation where stable enough to be meaningful
  - tokens/sec distribution summaries
  - run metadata (time range, sample count, canary config version)
- Baseline validity: baseline is considered valid only if it meets minimum sample size requirements (see below) and passes sanity checks (no telemetry gaps, no error-rate spikes).

### Rolling health baseline (calibration only)
- Window: trailing 7 days, excluding periods marked as “rollout” or “incident.”
- Aggregation: daily aggregates with robust statistics (median-of-daily-p95) to reduce sensitivity to short spikes.
- Purpose:
  - detect slow drift (e.g., fleet changes) that may warrant generating a new fixed baseline
  - suggest region/SKU overrides when variance is consistently higher

## Regression Definition
We define regression as a statistically and operationally meaningful deviation from the fixed baseline.

### Core comparison method (v1)
For each metric M and key K in each evaluation window W:
- Compute observed metric value O(K, W).
- Fetch baseline value B(K) and baseline variability estimates when available.
- Compute percent change: Δ = (O - B) / B.

We use percent change thresholds with guardrails (min samples + sustained breach). Percent thresholds are chosen because:
- Different models have different absolute latencies.
- p95 is the primary SLO-relevant tail metric.

### Threshold levels
Two alert levels exist:
- **Warning**: actionable signal for runtime team triage; routed to #eng-runtime.
- **Paging**: sustained, high-confidence regression on top models; routed to on-call rotation.

## Default Thresholds (v1)
Thresholds are configurable per model, with these platform defaults used unless overridden.

### Latency (p95 end-to-end)
- Warning: p95 E2E latency **+10%** vs baseline for **2 out of 3** consecutive evaluation windows.
- Paging: p95 E2E latency **+20%** vs baseline for **3 out of 4** consecutive evaluation windows.

### Throughput (tokens/sec)
- Warning: tokens/sec **-8%** vs baseline for **2 out of 3** windows.
- Paging: tokens/sec **-15%** vs baseline for **3 out of 4** windows.

### Runtime-only attribution signals (non-paging by default)
These are emitted to dashboards and included in alert payloads, but do not page unless explicitly enabled for a model:
- Queueing time p95 +25% (suggests scheduler/fleet saturation)
- Prefill p95 +15% (suggests kernel/model config regressions)
- Decode p95 +15% (suggests kernel/model config regressions)

Rationale:
- Paging should be driven by end-to-end customer-impact proxy (E2E p95 and tokens/sec).
- Breakdown metrics improve triage but are often noisier and more sensitive to load mix.

## Minimum Sample Sizes and Windowing
### Evaluation window
- Default evaluation window: **10 minutes**.
- Windows are evaluated continuously and require sustained breach rules (see anti-flap).

### Minimum samples (per key K, per window)
- **Latency metrics**: minimum **200 successful canary requests** per window.
- **Tokens/sec**: minimum **200 successful canary requests** per window AND minimum **50,000 total generated tokens** per window.

If sample requirements are not met:
- No alert state change is allowed (prevents flapping in low-coverage periods).
- Dashboard panels display “insufficient sample” annotation.

Rationale:
- Tail latency (p95/p99) is unreliable with very small N.
- Tokens/sec needs sufficient token volume to smooth per-request variance.

## Anti-Flap, Dedupe, and Hysteresis
### Sustained breach logic
We use “M out of N windows” to reduce sensitivity to transient spikes:
- Warning: 2/3 consecutive windows breach
- Paging: 3/4 consecutive windows breach

### Alert clear (hysteresis)
Alerts clear only when performance returns closer to baseline:
- Warning clears when Δ < +6% (latency) and Δ > -5% (tokens/sec) for 3/3 windows.
- Paging clears when Δ < +12% (latency) and Δ > -10% (tokens/sec) for 4/4 windows.

### Cooldown
After an alert transitions to firing, we enforce a cooldown before re-triggering a new incident thread for the same key:
- Warning cooldown: 60 minutes
- Paging cooldown: 120 minutes

### Dedupe key
Incident-bot dedupes based on:
- model_id + model_version + runtime_build_id + kernel_set_id + hardware_sku (+ region if present) + metric_type + severity

### Telemetry delay protection
If telemetry ingestion delay exceeds 2x the evaluation window (e.g., >20 minutes), alert evaluation is suspended and a separate “telemetry delay” warning is emitted to #eng-platform.

## Alternatives Considered
### A) Pure rolling baseline (always updated)
Pros:
- Adapts to drift without explicit re-baselining.
Cons:
- Risk of masking slow regressions (baseline chases the regression).
- Hard to use for rollout gating (“moving goalposts”).
- Susceptible to alert storms if rolling logic is impacted by label bugs or ingestion gaps.

Decision: Rejected for paging/gating. Allowed only as a secondary calibration signal.

### B) Pure fixed baseline (never updated unless manual)
Pros:
- Extremely stable; easy to reason about.
Cons:
- Becomes stale as fleet/hardware/driver changes accumulate; increases false positives over time.
- Forces frequent manual work to remain relevant.

Decision: Rejected as the only strategy. Adopted as primary baseline with explicit regeneration workflows.

### C) Per-region baselines always on
Pros:
- Reduces false positives due to cross-region fleet differences.
Cons:
- Baseline explosion (more artifacts and config), operational overhead.

Decision: Region is optional and only enabled for models/regions where observed variance requires it.

## Consequences
### Positive
- Stable, versioned baselines support rollout gates and high-confidence paging.
- Rolling health baseline provides drift visibility without undermining safety.
- Anti-flap controls reduce alert noise and prevent incident-bot storms.

### Negative / Tradeoffs
- Requires baseline artifact generation whenever major runtime/kernel changes land for covered models/SKUs.
- Some true regressions may be detected slightly later due to sustained-breach requirements.
- Optional region/tier overrides add complexity when enabled.

## Implementation Notes / Requirements
1. Baseline artifacts must be stored in an access-controlled, versioned store with auditability (who/when created).
2. Canary config must support per-model overrides:
   - thresholds by severity
   - whether region-specific keys are enabled
   - “top model” flag affecting paging eligibility
3. Alert payloads must include:
   - observed value, baseline value, percent change
   - sample counts and token counts
   - runtime_build_id, kernel_set_id, hardware_sku, region
   - links to dashboards, traces, and runbook
4. Changes to default thresholds must go through an ADR update or SRE-approved change request.

## Rollout Guidance
- Phase 1 (single region): warnings only, tune per-model overrides until noise is acceptable.
- Phase 2: enable paging only for top models with proven low variance and stable baselines.
- Phase 3: integrate gating with release engineering once paging signal is trusted (warning can gate early as “soft fail,” paging as “hard fail”).

## Open Questions (tracked outside ADR)
- Whether to introduce a scheduler_profile tag in baseline keys (cardinality vs attribution benefit).
- Whether to page on runtime-only p95 for certain enterprise-dedicated pools (when E2E has additional network variance).
- Whether to incorporate a lightweight statistical test (e.g., bootstrap CI on p95) for models with unusually high variance.

## Appendix A: Example Per-Model Overrides (illustrative)
- Top model (customer-critical): paging at +15% p95 E2E, -12% tokens/sec, stricter min token count.
- High-variance model: warning only, wider thresholds (+15% / -10%), region-specific baseline enabled.
- New kernel rollout candidate: temporary tighter warning (+8%) during rollout ring, but paging remains at default until validated.

## Appendix B: Ownership
- Baseline pipeline and artifact integrity: Telemetry Platform (Logan Wright) + Metrics/Baselines (Nadia Rahman)
- Threshold standards and paging hygiene: SRE (Sean Gallagher, Allison Grant)
- Model list and rollout gating integration: Serving Runtime (Noah Patel) + Release Engineering (Connor O'Brien)

## Changelog
- 2026-02-12: Clarified cooldown timers, added telemetry delay protection rule, and tightened tokens/sec minimum token count requirement.

title: ADR-0142: Perf Canary Baselines, Regression Thresholds, Min Sample Sizes, and Anti-Flap Logic
preview:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
This ADR defines how the production performance canary computes baselines and evaluates regressions for per-model serving performance (primarily p95 latency and tokens/sec). It documents:
- Baseline computation approach (rolling vs fixed) and when each is used.
- Regression thresholds (warning vs paging) and how they are applied.
- Minimum sample sizes and confidence requirements to avoid noisy alerts.
- Anti-flap / dedupe logic to prevent alert storms during drift, telemetry delays, or small-sample variance.

## Context / Problem
Redwood’s serving runtime changes frequently (kernel selection updates, scheduler heuristics, model config changes, GPU fleet changes, and staged rollouts). We need an always-on canary that:
- Detects real p95 latency regressions and throughput drops early.
- Produces actionable signals for attribution (kernel vs scheduler vs infra).
- Avoids paging on expected variance and baseline drift (e.g., slow fleet mix changes or regional differences).

The key design tension is baseline choice:
- A **fixed baseline** is stable and easy to reason about during rollouts, but can become stale as hardware/driver stacks and workloads evolve.
- A **rolling baseline** adapts over time, but can drift (masking regressions) or create false positives if drift is misinterpreted as regression.

## Decision Drivers
1. **Low noise / high trust**: warnings should be useful; pages should be rare and high-confidence.
2. **Fast detection**: detect regressions within ~10–30 minutes for top models in covered regions.
3. **Attribution**: baseline keys must align to dimensions needed for root cause (runtime build, kernel tags, SKU).
4. **Operational simplicity**: SRE/on-call should have a predictable interpretation of “regression vs baseline,” including during ongoing rollouts.
5. **Guardrails against drift and telemetry issues**: avoid alert storms caused by baseline drift, delayed metric ingestion, and label cardinality errors.

## Decision
We adopt a **hybrid baseline strategy**:

1) **Primary baseline = Fixed, versioned baseline per (model, model_version, runtime_build, kernel_set_id, hardware_sku) with optional region override**
- Baselines are generated from controlled canary runs (prod-like load) and stored as immutable artifacts.
- Baselines are explicitly tied to the runtime build and kernel selection identifiers so comparisons remain meaningful.
- This is the baseline used for rollout gating and for paging.

2) **Secondary baseline = Rolling “health baseline” used for drift monitoring and threshold calibration (not for paging)**
- We compute a rolling reference from recent stable periods (default: last 7 days) to detect slow environmental drift and to inform recalibration.
- Rolling baseline is not used to auto-suppress a fixed-baseline regression; it is used as a triage and tuning signal.

3) **Baseline freeze rules**
- During a runtime rollout (canary/gradual), the baseline used for alerts is **frozen** to the most recent fixed baseline for the currently active runtime build on that ring.
- If an incident is declared for a perf regression, baseline updates are **paused** for affected keys until incident closure.

4) **Threshold evaluation requires minimum sample size + sustained breach + hysteresis**
- Alerts require both a minimum number of canary requests and sustained breach across multiple evaluation windows.
- Alert clear conditions are stricter than trigger conditions (hysteresis) to reduce flapping.

## Baseline Keys (What defines “like-for-like”)
Baseline artifacts and regression evaluation are keyed by the following dimensions:

**Required baseline key fields (v1):**
- model_id (canonical model identifier)
- model_version (registry version / hash)
- runtime_build_id (serving runtime build tag)
- kernel_set_id (kernel selection/version identifier)
- hardware_sku (e.g., H100-80GB, A100-80GB)

**Optional fields (only when variance requires it):**
- region (enabled for regions with materially different fleet/network characteristics)
- capacity_tier (hosted vs dedicated pools) when pools differ materially

Notes:
- We do NOT key baselines by customer, endpoint, or prompt content (privacy + cardinality constraints).
- Scheduler configuration flags that materially change behavior must be reflected either in runtime_build_id or an explicit scheduler_profile tag (introduced only if needed; kept off by default to control cardinality).

## Metrics Covered
Baselines and regressions are computed for:
- End-to-end latency: p50, p95, p99 (ms)
- Runtime-only latency: p50, p95, p99 (ms) (excludes network/client)
- Prefill latency and decode latency (ms)
- Queueing time (ms) (scheduler-level)
- Tokens/sec (overall, and decode tokens/sec where available)
- Batch size distribution (for attribution)
- KV cache hit rate (where applicable; informational at v1)

## Baseline Computation
### Fixed baseline artifact
- Source: controlled perf-canary runs using the approved synthetic prompt distribution.
- For each baseline key, we store:
  - distribution summaries (p50/p95/p99) for each latency metric
  - mean and standard deviation where stable enough to be meaningful
  - tokens/sec distribution summaries
  - run metadata (time range, sample count, canary config version)
- Baseline validity: baseline is considered valid only if it meets minimum sample size requirements (see below) and passes sanity checks (no telemetry gaps, no error-rate spikes).

### Rolling health baseline (calibration only)
- Window: trailing 7 days, excluding periods marked as “rollout” or “incident.”
- Aggregation: daily aggregates with robust statistics (median-of-daily-p95) to reduce sensitivity to short spikes.
- Purpose:
  - detect slow drift (e.g., fleet changes) that may warrant generating a new fixed baseline
  - suggest region/SKU overrides when variance is consistently higher

## Regression Definition
We define regression as a statistically and operationally meaningful deviation from the fixed baseline.

### Core comparison method (v1)
For each metric M and key K in each evaluation window W:
- Compute observed metric value O(K, W).
- Fetch baseline value B(K) and baseline variability estimates when available.
- Compute percent change: Δ = (O - B) / B.

We use percent change thresholds with guardrails (min samples + sustained breach). Percent thresholds are chosen because:
- Different models have different absolute latencies.
- p95 is the primary SLO-relevant tail metric.

### Threshold levels
Two alert levels exist:
- **Warning**: actionable signal for runtime team triage; routed to #eng-runtime.
- **Paging**: sustained, high-confidence regression on top models; routed to on-call rotation.

## Default Thresholds (v1)
Thresholds are configurable per model, with these platform defaults used unless overridden.

### Latency (p95 end-to-end)
- Warning: p95 E2E latency **+10%** vs baseline for **2 out of 3** consecutive evaluation windows.
- Paging: p95 E2E latency **+20%** vs baseline for **3 out of 4** consecutive evaluation windows.

### Throughput (tokens/sec)
- Warning: tokens/sec **-8%** vs baseline for **2 out of 3** windows.
- Paging: tokens/sec **-15%** vs baseline for **3 out of 4** windows.

### Runtime-only attribution signals (non-paging by default)
These are emitted to dashboards and included in alert payloads, but do not page unless explicitly enabled for a model:
- Queueing time p95 +25% (suggests scheduler/fleet saturation)
- Prefill p95 +15% (suggests kernel/model config regressions)
- Decode p95 +15% (suggests kernel/model config regressions)

Rationale:
- Paging should be driven by end-to-end customer-impact proxy (E2E p95 and tokens/sec).
- Breakdown metrics improve triage but are often noisier and more sensitive to load mix.

## Minimum Sample Sizes and Windowing
### Evaluation window
- Default evaluation window: **10 minutes**.
- Windows are evaluated continuously and require sustained breach rules (see anti-flap).

### Minimum samples (per key K, per window)
- **Latency metrics**: minimum **200 successful canary requests** per window.
- **Tokens/sec**: minimum **200 successful canary requests** per window AND minimum **50,000 total generated tokens** per window.

If sample requirements are not met:
- No alert state change is allowed (prevents flapping in low-coverage periods).
- Dashboard panels display “insufficient sample” annotation.

Rationale:
- Tail latency (p95/p99) is unreliable with very small N.
- Tokens/sec needs sufficient token volume to smooth per-request variance.

## Anti-Flap, Dedupe, and Hysteresis
### Sustained breach logic
We use “M out of N windows” to reduce sensitivity to transient spikes:
- Warning: 2/3 consecutive windows breach
- Paging: 3/4 consecutive windows breach

### Alert clear (hysteresis)
Alerts clear only when performance returns closer to baseline:
- Warning clears when Δ < +6% (latency) and Δ > -5% (tokens/sec) for 3/3 windows.
- Paging clears when Δ < +12% (latency) and Δ > -10% (tokens/sec) for 4/4 windows.

### Cooldown
After an alert transitions to firing, we enforce a cooldown before re-triggering a new incident thread for the same key:
- Warning cooldown: 60 minutes
- Paging cooldown: 120 minutes

### Dedupe key
Incident-bot dedupes based on:
- model_id + model_version + runtime_build_id + kernel_set_id + hardware_sku (+ region if present) + metric_type + severity

### Telemetry delay protection
If telemetry ingestion delay exceeds 2x the evaluation window (e.g., >20 minutes), alert evaluation is suspended and a separate “telemetry delay” warning is emitted to #eng-platform.

## Alternatives Considered
### A) Pure rolling baseline (always updated)
Pros:
- Adapts to drift without explicit re-baselining.
Cons:
- Risk of masking slow regressions (baseline chases the regression).
- Hard to use for rollout gating (“moving goalposts”).
- Susceptible to alert storms if rolling logic is impacted by label bugs or ingestion gaps.

Decision: Rejected for paging/gating. Allowed only as a secondary calibration signal.

### B) Pure fixed baseline (never updated unless manual)
Pros:
- Extremely stable; easy to reason about.
Cons:
- Becomes stale as fleet/hardware/driver changes accumulate; increases false positives over time.
- Forces frequent manual work to remain relevant.

Decision: Rejected as the only strategy. Adopted as primary baseline with explicit regeneration workflows.

### C) Per-region baselines always on
Pros:
- Reduces false positives due to cross-region fleet differences.
Cons:
- Baseline explosion (more artifacts and config), operational overhead.

Decision: Region is optional and only enabled for models/regions where observed variance requires it.

## Consequences
### Positive
- Stable, versioned baselines support rollout gates and high-confidence paging.
- Rolling health baseline provides drift visibility without undermining safety.
- Anti-flap controls reduce alert noise and prevent incident-bot storms.

### Negative / Tradeoffs
- Requires baseline artifact generation whenever major runtime/kernel changes land for covered models/SKUs.
- Some true regressions may be detected slightly later due to sustained-breach requirements.
- Optional region/tier overrides add complexity when enabled.

## Implementation Notes / Requirements
1. Baseline artifacts must be stored in an access-controlled, versioned store with auditability (who/when created).
2. Canary config must support per-model overrides:
   - thresholds by severity
   - whether region-specific keys are enabled
   - “top model” flag affecting paging eligibility
3. Alert payloads must include:
   - observed value, baseline value, percent change
   - sample counts and token counts
   - runtime_build_id, kernel_set_id, hardware_sku, region
   - links to dashboards, traces, and runbook
4. Changes to default thresholds must go through an ADR update or SRE-approved change request.

## Rollout Guidance
- Phase 1 (single region): warnings only, tune per-model overrides until noise is acceptable.
- Phase 2: enable paging only for top models with proven low variance and stable baselines.
- Phase 3: integrate gating with release engineering once paging signal is trusted (warning can gate early as “soft fail,” paging as “hard fail”).

## Open Questions (tracked outside ADR)
- Whether to introduce a scheduler_profile tag in baseline keys (cardinality vs attribution benefit).
- Whether to page on runtime-only p95 for certain enterprise-dedicated pools (when E2E has additional network variance).
- Whether to incorporate a lightweight statistical test (e.g., bootstrap CI on p95) for models with unusually high variance.

## Appendix A: Example Per-Model Overrides (illustrative)
- Top model (customer-critical): paging at +15% p95 E2E, -12% tokens/sec, stricter min token count.
- High-variance model: warning only, wider thresholds (+15% / -10%), region-specific baseline enabled.
- New kernel rollout candidate: temporary tighter warning (+8%) during rollout ring, but paging remains at default until validated.

## Appendix B: Ownership
- Baseline pipeline and artifact integrity: Telemetry Platform (Logan Wright) + Metrics/Baselines (Nadia Rahman)
- Threshold standards and paging hygiene: SRE (Sean Gallagher, Allison Grant)
- Model list and rollout gating integration: Serving Runtime (Noah Patel) + Release Engineering (Connor O'Brien)

## Changelog
- 2026-02-12: Clarified cooldown timers, added telemetry delay protection rule, and tightened tokens/sec minimum token count requirement.

## Baseline Keys (What defines “like-for-like”)
Baseline artifacts and regression evaluation are keyed by the following dimensions:

**Required baseline key fields (v1):**
- model_id (canonical model identifier)
- model_version (registry version / hash)

- Scheduler configuration flags that materially change behavior must be reflected either in runtime_build_id or an explicit scheduler_profile tag (introduced only if needed; kept off by default to control cardinality).

- End-to-end latency: p50, p95, p99 (ms)
- Runtime-only latency: p50, p95, p99 (ms) (excludes network/client)

- Tokens/sec (overall, and decode tokens/sec where available)

- KV cache hit rate (where applicable; informational at v1)

## Baseline Computation
### Fixed baseline artifact
- Source: controlled perf-canary runs using the approved synthetic prompt distribution.
- For each baseline key, we store:
  - distribution summaries (p50/p95/p99) for each latency metric
  - mean and standard deviation where stable enough to be meaningful

### Rolling health baseline (calibration only)
- Window: trailing 7 days, excluding periods marked as “rollout” or “incident.”

- Purpose:
  - detect slow drift (e.g., fleet changes) that may warrant generating a new fixed baseline

We define regression as a statistically and operationally meaningful deviation from the fixed baseline.

- Compute observed metric value O(K, W).
- Fetch baseline value B(K) and baseline variability estimates when available.

- p95 is the primary SLO-relevant tail metric.

### Threshold levels
Two alert levels exist:
- **Warning**: actionable signal for runtime team triage; routed to #eng-runtime.
- **Paging**: sustained, high-confidence regression on top models; routed to on-call rotation.

## Default Thresholds (v1)
Thresholds are configurable per model, with these platform defaults used unless overridden.

### Latency (p95 end-to-end)
- Warning: p95 E2E latency **+10%** vs baseline for **2 out of 3** consecutive evaluation windows.
- Paging: p95 E2E latency **+20%** vs baseline for **3 out of 4** consecutive evaluation windows.

### Throughput (tokens/sec)
- Warning: tokens/sec **-8%** vs baseline for **2 out of 3** windows.
- Paging: tokens/sec **-15%** vs baseline for **3 out of 4** windows.

### Runtime-only attribution signals (non-paging by default)
These are emitted to dashboards and included in alert payloads, but do not page unless explicitly enabled for a model:
- Queueing time p95 +25% (suggests scheduler/fleet saturation)
- Prefill p95 +15% (suggests kernel/model config regressions)

Rationale:
- Paging should be driven by end-to-end customer-impact proxy (E2E p95 and tokens/sec).
- Breakdown metrics improve triage but are often noisier and more sensitive to load mix.

## Minimum Sample Sizes and Windowing
### Evaluation window
- Default evaluation window: **10 minutes**.
- Windows are evaluated continuously and require sustained breach rules (see anti-flap).

### Minimum samples (per key K, per window)
- **Latency metrics**: minimum **200 successful canary requests** per window.
- **Tokens/sec**: minimum **200 successful canary requests** per window AND minimum **50,000 total generated tokens** per window.

If sample requirements are not met:
- No alert state change is allowed (prevents flapping in low-coverage periods).
- Dashboard panels display “insufficient sample” annotation.

Rationale:
- Tail latency (p95/p99) is unreliable with very small N.
- Tokens/sec needs sufficient token volume to smooth per-request variance.

## Anti-Flap, Dedupe, and Hysteresis
### Sustained breach logic
We use “M out of N windows” to reduce sensitivity to transient spikes:
- Warning: 2/3 consecutive windows breach
- Paging: 3/4 consecutive windows breach

### Alert clear (hysteresis)
Alerts clear only when performance returns closer to baseline:
- Warning clears when Δ < +6% (latency) and Δ > -5% (tokens/sec) for 3/3 windows.
- Paging clears when Δ < +12% (latency) and Δ > -10% (tokens/sec) for 4/4 windows.

### Cooldown
After an alert transitions to firing, we enforce a cooldown before re-triggering a new incident thread for the same key:
- Warning cooldown: 60 minutes
- Paging cooldown: 120 minutes

### Dedupe key
Incident-bot dedupes based on:
- model_id + model_version + runtime_build_id + kernel_set_id + hardware_sku (+ region if present) + metric_type + severity

### Telemetry delay protection
If telemetry ingestion delay exceeds 2x the evaluation window (e.g., >20 minutes), alert evaluation is suspended and a separate “telemetry delay” warning is emitted to #eng-platform.

## Alternatives Considered
### A) Pure rolling baseline (always updated)
Pros:
- Adapts to drift without explicit re-baselining.
Cons:
- Risk of masking slow regressions (baseline chases the regression).
- Hard to use for rollout gating (“moving goalposts”).
- Susceptible to alert storms if rolling logic is impacted by label bugs or ingestion gaps.

### B) Pure fixed baseline (never updated unless manual)
Pros:
- Extremely stable; easy to reason about.
Cons:
- Becomes stale as fleet/hardware/driver changes accumulate; increases false positives over time.

Decision: Rejected as the only strategy. Adopted as primary baseline with explicit regeneration workflows.

1. Baseline artifacts must be stored in an access-controlled, versioned store with auditability (who/when created).
2. Canary config must support per-model overrides:
