- If server includes retry_after_seconds, add that to the computed wait and respect it as a floor.

6) Quality fallback strategies
- If MODEL_DEGRADATION is signaled, server MAY include the active variant and a suggested lower-cost variant in the error payload. Client options:
  a) surface degraded output with UI hint; do not retry automatically;
  b) call an explicit \"regenerate\" with higher quality variant if user requests and the client can pay.

Observability and telemetry requirements:
- Every terminal event for a stream must emit metrics and traces with these fields: stream_id, request_id, user_id (if available), model_variant, kernel_build, node_shard, stream_error_type, resume_token_present (bool), duration_ms, bytes_sent, bytes_received.
- SLIs to track (per-service and per-model-variant):
  - streaming_success_rate (target: 99.5% p99 over 1h)
  - resume_success_rate (target: 99% p95 over 24h)
  - incremental_retries_due_to_network (target: <0.5% of requests)
  - tail_stream_latency_p99 (service-specific budgets; default 2.5s for single-response-turn streams)

Testing, verification, and rollout checklist:
1) Unit tests: error payload is emitted and parsable for all close paths.
2) Integration tests: simulate network drop, partial frames, and CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH to verify client behavior in SDK tests.
3) Load tests: measure impact of retries under 2x, 5x failure scenarios and assert tail latency remains within budget when retries occur.
4) Canary rollout: enable streaming error payloads on a narrow user segment (5% traffic) for 72h, monitor resume_success_rate and streaming_success_rate.
5) Gradual expansion: 5% -> 25% -> 75% -> 100% with 24h observation windows and automated rollback on SLO degradation.

Runbook excerpts (for SRE):
- Symptom: large spike in TRANSIENT_NETWORK closures
  1) Query traces for stream_error_type = TRANSIENT_NETWORK and filter by node_shard.
  2) If >5% of streams in last 5m originate from a single shard, evacuate shard and check recent kernel_release changes.
  3) If resume tokens are present but resume_success_rate < 90%, extend token retention window temporarily and eject suspect kernel build.

- Symptom: elevated CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH errors
  1) Identify latest schema or checkpoint encoding changes via artifact release logs.
  2) If change is recent, roll back the release to previous artifact and escalate to runtime team.

Implementation recipes and examples:
- Example streaming close header (HTTP chunked):
  Trailer: x-stream-error
  x-stream-error: {\"stream_error_type\":\"RATE_LIMITED\",\"message\":\"burst quota exceeded\",\"retry_after_seconds\":30,\"request_id\":\"req-2026-01-21-11\"}

- Example gRPC trailing metadata (pseudo):
  grpc-status: 8
  x-stream-error-bin: <base64(json-payload)>

- Example client pseudo-code for resume attempt:
  1) onStreamClose(meta):
     if meta.x-stream-error.resume_token:
       for attempt in 1..3:
         wait(randomized_backoff(attempt))
         resp = openStream({resumeToken: meta.resume_token})
         if resp.ok: attach and continue
       surface \"Resume failed\" and fall back to full restart
     else: full restart or surface to user

Enforcement and governance:
- API gate: all streaming endpoints must implement the x-stream-error payload by Q3 2026. Non-compliant services will be flagged by the nightly API conformance job.
- SDKs: official Redwood SDKs will implement default retry logic conforming to this contract; third-party clients are encouraged but not forced to use SDKs.
- Auditing: Weekly reports will summarize top stream_error_types by service and client platform; Product and SRE will review during the reliability sync.

Ownership and contacts:
- Specification owner: Priya Natarajan (eng/platform)
- Runtime/implementation owner: Runtime Team (eng-serving-runtime) – primary contact: Ethan Park
- Observability owner: Platform SRE – primary contact: Marta Silva
- Rollout coordinator: Product Infrastructure – Samira Ahmed

Appendix: change log
- 2025-09-17: Drafted initial taxonomy and payload schema (Priya).
- 2025-11-10: Added resume token retention and TTL guidance (Ethan).
- 2026-02-08: Finalized SLI targets and rollout checklist; changed base backoff to 200ms (Marta).

References:
- API compatibility and deprecation policy (see related_pages)
- Partial result checkpointing and client resume playbook (see related_pages)

Feedback and revision process:
- Send suggested edits as PRs to the spec repo and include a short impact note. Major changes (affecting client retry behavior or resume semantics) require reviewers from Runtime, SRE, and SDK teams and a two-week review period.

source.title: Client Contracts for Structured Tool Calls and Retry Semantics
source.author: Priya Nair
source.summary: Guidelines and decision rationale for the client-side contract used when making structured tool calls (function/tool invocation) to Redwood inference endpoints, including envelope format, idempotency semantics, retry windows, and telemetry signals required for safe and cost-predictable retries.
source.body:
Overview:

This document specifies a cross-cutting client-side contract for making structured tool calls (a.k.a. function or tool invocation) via Redwood inference APIs. It describes: the JSON/YAML envelope, required fields, idempotency and retry semantics, backoff guidance, failure modes and mitigation, observability signals clients must emit, and recommended server-side acceptance tests. The intent is to reduce incorrect duplicate tool executions, limit surprise billing, and make retries diagnosable across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Why this exists:
- Tool calls can trigger side effects (webhooks, DB writes, external API calls). Poorly specified retries lead to duplicated side effects or inconsistent state.
- LLM tool-calling increases per-request token usage (function args, structured outputs). Clients should be able to reason about safe retry windows and idempotency.
- Observability and request metadata are required for post-incident triage and cost allocation.

Scope and non-goals:
- Scope: client contract and minimal server acceptance expectations for structured tool calls on Redwood inference endpoints. Includes offline SDK behavior and recommended tests for SDKs.
- Non-goal: a full two-phase commit protocol or distributed transactions for customer tool endpoints. We assume many destination tools are external and uncontrolled.

Contract: request envelope (JSON)
- Required top-level fields (example):
  {
    "request_id": "uuid-v4",
    "client_id": "string (project or org id)",
    "model": "string",
    "prompt": "string",
    "tool_call": {
      "tool_name": "string",
      "inputs": { /* arbitrary JSON */ },
      "expected_response_schema": "json-schema-v4-string (optional)"
    },
    "idempotency_key": "optional-string",
    "created_at": "RFC3339 timestamp",
    "safety_hints": ["no-exec", "dry-run"]
  }

- Semantics: request_id is our canonical correlation id that must be unique per logical client request. idempotency_key is a client-chosen stable key that indicates the same logical operation (e.g., create-invoice-123). If idempotency_key is present, Redwood must make best-effort deduplication within the configured idempotency window (see below).

Idempotency window and guarantees:
- Default idempotency window: 5 minutes for hosted endpoints (configurable per-customer for Dedicated/Private).
- Guarantee: If two requests arrive with the same idempotency_key within the idempotency window, Redwood will return the cached response (or the same success/failure disposition) for non-streaming tool-calls. This is an at-most-once best-effort guarantee for side-effecting tool-calls; clients should not rely on absolute deduplication for longer-lived operations.
- Stronger options: customers can request extended deduplication (up to 7 days) for Dedicated/Private via per-account config with associated storage and billing implications.

Retry classification (client guidance):
1) Safe to retry immediately (no idempotency concerns): idempotent read-only tool calls (e.g., lookup-by-id), or calls marked with safety_hints: ["dry-run", "no-exec"].
2) Retry with deduplication: calls with idempotency_key present. Client may retry with exponential backoff but must preserve idempotency_key.
3) Unsafe to retry automatically: tool calls that mutate external systems without idempotency support. Clients must use human confirmation, manual dedup keys, or server-side orchestration to avoid duplication.

Backoff and retry windows (recommended):
- Immediate: 200ms jittered initial delay for transient network failures (TCP connection reset, short timeout).
- Exponential backoff: factor 2, max backoff 30s for single request. Give up after 5 attempts unless idempotency_key is present and client has explicit reason to continue.
- Token-cost-aware retry: SDKs MUST avoid unbounded rapid retries for streaming responses — track tokens consumed on partial responses and surface partial tokens to clients before retrying.

Server-side acceptance tests (minimum):
1) Idempotency key echo: server must return an idempotency_status field indicating: ["accepted", "duplicate", "expired", "unsupported"].
2) Request correlation headers: server returns request_id, server_received_at, and a dedup_cache_hit boolean.
3) Safety hint enforcement: if client sets safety_hints: ["dry-run"], server must not perform external side effects and should label outputs with dry_run: true.

Telemetry and logging requirements (client and server):
- Clients must attach these fields to telemetry events: client_id, sdk_version, idempotency_key (if set), request_id, tool_name, estimated_token_budget.
- Servers must record: received_at, processing_latency_ms, idempotency_status, output_token_count, external_tool_invocations (count), and a trace_id mapping to distributed traces.
- Labeling convention: all telemetry fields MUST use snake_case keys.

Failure modes and recommended mitigations:
- Duplicate execution: encourage use of idempotency_key. For legacy clients, server-side replay-detection heuristics may be applied (best-effort).
- Partial streaming / truncated outputs: SDKs should expose truncated = true and partial_token_count to the caller. Automatic retries are allowed only if client confirms they accept truncated results; otherwise surface an error with code PARTIAL_OUTPUT.
- Billing surprises: server must report output_token_count and bill only for tokens accepted by the client at acknowledgement time (see billing policy linked below).

Client SDK checklist (minimum implementation for Redwood official SDKs):
1) Provide automatic idempotency_key generation for common operations and allow client override.
2) Expose telemetry hooks allowing customers to forward native telemetry to their systems.
3) Surface partial-stream events with token counts and a simple API to either commit, retry, or discard.
4) Implement retry backoff policy above and make it configurable.

Compatibility and rollout plan:
- Phase 0 (internal testing): SDK + server implement idempotency_status echo and minimal telemetry. Run against integration testbed.
- Phase 1 (opt-in beta): Add idempotency_key support to hosted endpoints behind a feature flag. Invite 10 trust accounts.
- Phase 2 (default): Gradual ramp to 100% of new endpoints over 4 weeks. Deprecate previous ambiguous retry semantics.

Operational runbook snippets (on-call triggers):
- Trigger: sharp increase in external_tool_invocations combined with dedup_cache_hit rate < 1% for requests with idempotency_key.
  - Action: verify idempotency store health, check storage latency, rollback recent change to dedup index writes, notify integrators.
- Trigger: increase in PARTIAL_OUTPUT errors > 0.5% of requests over 1h.
  - Action: examine streaming backend latency, verify kernel memory pressure, check client SDK releases for changed read buffer sizes.

Acceptance tests (CI) for SDKs and server: (table)
- Test name | Purpose | Expected result
- idempotency-duplicate | ensure same idempotency_key returns duplicate status | duplicate returned, no second external invocation
- dry-run-safety | ensure dry-run prevents side effects | external_tool_invocations == 0, dry_run == true in response
- streaming-truncation | simulate partial chunk then abort | SDK surfaces truncated=true and partial_token_count > 0

Security and privacy notes:
- Idempotency keys must not contain PII. SDKs must sanitize common inputs before using them as idempotency keys.
- Telemetry fields that may contain user content must be hashed before transmission per privacy policy.

Open decisions (ADRs to follow):
- ADR 1: long-term storage duration for deduplication keys for Dedicated/Private (trade-offs: cost vs. customer safety).
- ADR 2: canonical token accounting for partially streamed responses (how many tokens billed on retry).

References and related docs:
- /confluence/architecture-and-standards/api-standards/request-semantics-and-cost-aware-payload-guidelines-2026
- /confluence/architecture-and-standards/model-serving-standards/structured-response-contract-testing-guidelines-2026
- Billing policy (internal link): /confluence/finance-and-legal/revrec-and-billing/token-billing-and-partial-response-policy-2025

Appendix: example server response (minimal):
{
  "request_id": "uuid-v4",
  "status": "ok",
  "idempotency_status": "accepted|duplicate|expired|unsupported",
  "server_received_at": "2026-02-08T18:42:00Z",
  "dedup_cache_hit": false,
  "output_token_count": "42",
  "external_tool_invocations": 1,
  "dry_run": false
}

Change log:
- 2025-10-12: created (Priya Nair)
- 2025-12-01: reviewer feedback incorporated, expanded telemetry requirements (E. Park)
- 2026-02-08: clarified billing and partial output behavior (L. Gómez)

Owners and contacts:
- Primary owner: priya.nair@redwood.ai
- Secondary: eng-api@redwood.ai

source.title: ADR-0142: Tier-aware SLI tagging at ingress (Hosted vs Dedicated)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.content:
## Status
Published (effective for SLO reporting + error budget enforcement starting Q1 2026 rollout).

## Context / Problem
Redwood is formalizing tiered SLOs and error budgets for **Hosted (shared)** vs **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**. We must be able to compute SLIs (success rate, latency percentiles, streaming reliability) with correct tier attribution across:
- API Gateway handling (auth, rate limits, request normalization)
- Smart Routing (policy evaluation, fallback, retries)
- Model serving request path (batching, execution)
- Observability pipelines (metrics, logs, traces)

We have recurring reliability escalations tied to burst traffic where:
- Requests may be **retried** or **routed to fallback capacity**.
- Failures can occur at multiple layers (gateway, routing, serving, stream transport).
- Dedicated customers need **account-level visibility**, while Hosted must avoid **metric cardinality explosions**.

Prior to this ADR, tier attribution was inconsistent:
- Some metrics inferred tier from where the request was executed (e.g., which GPU pool), which breaks when fallback occurs.
- Some services did not propagate tier context, causing “unknown” attribution and gaps in SLO accounting.

**Requirement:** Tier attribution must reflect the customer’s contracted service tier (Hosted vs Dedicated), not the execution path chosen at runtime, and must be preserved end-to-end so that SLO/error budget calculations are correct and explainable.

## Decision
We will **attach a tier-aware SLI tag set at ingress (API Gateway)** and **propagate it through routing, retries, fallback, and serving** via request context, trace attributes, and metric labels.

Specifically:
1) The **API Gateway (edge-gw)** is the source of truth for tier classification. It sets tier tags immediately after authentication and before request dispatch.
2) The tier tags are treated as **immutable** for the lifetime of the request (including internal retries). Downstream systems may add analysis tags (e.g., where it executed) but **must not overwrite the ingress tier**.
3) SLI metrics used for SLO/error budgets will be computed primarily from gateway + routing boundary metrics, with tier tags included as first-class dimensions.

## Tier tag set (canonical)
### Required tags (present on all in-scope request metrics)
- `slo_tier`: one of `hosted` | `dedicated` | `unknown`
  - Meaning: tier to be used for SLO/error budget accounting.
- `endpoint_class`: one of `chat` | `completions` | `embeddings` | `rerank` | `other`
  - Meaning: stable grouping used for SLO targets and dashboards.
- `region`: standard Redwood region identifier (e.g., `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`).
- `streaming`: `true` | `false`

### Optional tags (allowed, with constraints)
- `dedicated_account_id`: stable internal account identifier (string).
  - **Only emitted for `slo_tier=dedicated`** and only on an allowlisted metric set used by Dedicated SLO dashboards.
  - Must not be emitted for Hosted to avoid cardinality blowups.
- `execution_tier`: `dedicated_pool` | `hosted_pool` | `fallback_pool` | `unknown`
  - Meaning: where the request actually executed. Used for analysis and mitigation; **not used** for SLO accounting.
- `fallback_reason`: small controlled vocabulary (e.g., `capacity`, `health`, `policy`, `region_failover`).
  - Must be rate-limited and taxonomy-controlled.

### Explicit non-goals / forbidden in high-volume metrics
- No per-model, per-customer, per-api-key dimensions on gateway request counters/histograms (except the Dedicated allowlist noted above).
- No raw customer names/slugs in metric labels.

## Propagation rules
### Ingress tagging (edge-gw)
- edge-gw resolves tier using the authenticated principal (API key / token) via Control Plane metadata.
- edge-gw attaches tier tags to:
  - internal request context (headers or metadata, service-to-service)
  - trace context (OTel attributes / baggage)
  - gateway metrics (Prometheus labels / OTLP metric attributes)

### Routing and fallback
- Routing **must preserve `slo_tier`** from the ingress context.
- If routing chooses fallback execution (e.g., Dedicated spilling to a compatible pool), it adds `execution_tier` and `fallback_reason` but does not change `slo_tier`.
- If tier is missing or `unknown`, routing does not attempt to infer tier from pool selection for SLO purposes.

### Retries
- Internal retries (gateway or routing) keep the same `slo_tier`.
- SLI accounting rules for retries are handled in the SLI specification (e.g., whether we count request-level vs attempt-level failures), but tier tags must be consistent across attempts.

### Streaming
- For streaming requests, `slo_tier` must be present on:
  - stream-open events
  - stream-close events
  - disconnect/error events
- Disconnection events must preserve the original tier even if subsequent fallback is attempted (e.g., reconnect behavior).

## Options considered
### Option A (Chosen): Tag at ingress and propagate
Pros:
- Single source of truth for tier attribution.
- Correct under fallback, retries, and multi-hop execution.
- Matches customer expectation: tier is a contract/property of the customer, not of a GPU pool.
Cons:
- Requires coordinated plumbing across gateway, routing, telemetry, and dashboards.

### Option B: Infer tier from execution pool
Pros:
- Simplifies tagging for serving/runtime.
Cons:
- Incorrect when Dedicated falls back to shared capacity.
- Breaks SLO accounting and customer reporting; leads to under/over-counting dedicated impact.

### Option C: Determine tier in routing (not gateway)
Pros:
- Routing already has policy context.
Cons:
- Gateway-level failures (auth proxying, overload shedding, request parsing, upstream 5xx) would be un-attributed.
- Adds complexity during partial outages when routing metadata is unavailable.

## Rationale
Tiered SLOs and error budgets require:
- **Correct attribution** even when the system behaves dynamically (fallback, retries, shifting regions).
- **Consistency** across metrics, traces, and logs.
- **Controlled cardinality** to keep observability cost and reliability manageable.

Ingress (edge-gw) is the only location that:
- Always sees the request for in-scope endpoints.
- Has stable access to identity metadata (API key → account → tier).
- Can tag before any failure modes in routing/serving occur.

## Implementation details
### Source of tier truth
- Control Plane will expose a simple mapping for authenticated principals:
  - `principal_id` → `account_id`, `deployment_mode`, `slo_tier`
- Canonical mapping:
  - Hosted API keys → `slo_tier=hosted`
  - Dedicated API keys (or Dedicated sub-accounts) → `slo_tier=dedicated`
  - Any unrecognized/malformed/legacy principals → `slo_tier=unknown`

### Data model notes
- `deployment_mode` may exist internally (e.g., hosted_shared, dedicated_reserved, private_vpc), but SLO scope for this program is `hosted` and `dedicated`. `private` is out of enforcement scope and will map to `unknown` or a future value once included.

### Telemetry constraints (cardinality)
- Gateway request metrics will include only low-cardinality dimensions by default.
- Dedicated per-account SLO dashboards require `dedicated_account_id` on a narrow set of metrics (request totals, error totals, latency histograms) and only when `slo_tier=dedicated`.
- Hosted will not include any customer identifiers in metrics; customer-level troubleshooting relies on logs/traces and sampled analytics.

### Validation and rollout
- SLO toolkit validation will require that all in-scope services emit `slo_tier` on the canonical metric series.
- Rollout is phased:
  1. Warn-only: `unknown` tier is tracked and surfaced on dashboards; no enforcement.
  2. Soft gating: missing tier tags block new SLO configs for affected endpoint/region.
  3. Hard requirement: SLO/error budget reporting excludes `unknown` traffic from tier-specific dashboards and flags as a data-quality incident when above threshold.

### Data-quality SLO for tagging
We will track:
- `% requests with slo_tier != unknown` by endpoint_class/region.
Target:
- Dedicated: >= 99.99% correctly tagged
- Hosted: >= 99.9% correctly tagged

## Consequences
### Positive
- Accurate and explainable tier-specific SLO/error budget accounting.
- Enables Dedicated customer reporting without polluting Hosted metrics.
- Improves incident triage: responders can immediately see if an event is tier-scoped.

### Negative / trade-offs
- Requires careful coordination to ensure tags survive all hops (gateway → routing → serving → telemetry).
- Dedicated per-account metrics introduce bounded cardinality; must be actively managed (allowlist, relabeling, and periodic audits).

## Security / Privacy considerations
- `dedicated_account_id` is an internal identifier and must not expose customer names.
- Any customer-identifying tags must not be emitted on public/shared dashboards.
- Access to Dedicated account dashboards is controlled via RBAC (Console + Grafana).

## Open questions
- Private tier tagging strategy once Private is included in enforcement (future ADR).
- Exact treatment of multi-account keys and sub-tenant rollups for Dedicated customers with multiple environments.

## Action items
- Implement ingress tier tagging and propagation (edge-gw, routing context, OTel trace propagation). (Owner: Amara Diallo / Mina Haddad)
- Update dashboards and recording rules to require `slo_tier`. (Owner: Sergio Costa)
- Add SLO toolkit validation for metric coverage and missing tier tags. (Owner: Logan Wright)
- Document SLI formulas and exclusions with tier semantics, including fallback accounting. (Owner: Aisha Bello)

## Appendix: Controlled vocabularies
- `slo_tier`: `hosted`, `dedicated`, `unknown`
- `execution_tier`: `dedicated_pool`, `hosted_pool`, `fallback_pool`, `unknown`
- `fallback_reason` (initial): `capacity`, `health`, `policy`, `region_failover`

---
Changelog:
- 2026-02-06: Clarified dedicated per-account metric allowlist and introduced `execution_tier` as analysis-only.
- 2026-02-02: Added data-quality targets for `% tagged` coverage and phased rollout notes.

Tiered SLOs and error budgets require:
- **Correct attribution** even when the system behaves dynamically (fallback, retries, shifting regions).
- **Consistency** across metrics, traces, and logs.
- **Controlled cardinality** to keep observability cost and reliability manageable.

Ingress (edge-gw) is the only location that:
- Always sees the request for in-scope endpoints.
- Has stable access to identity metadata (API key → account → tier).
- Can tag before any failure modes in routing/serving occur.

## Implementation details
### Source of tier truth
- Control Plane will expose a simple mapping for authenticated principals:
  - `principal_id` → `account_id`, `deployment_mode`, `slo_tier`
- Canonical mapping:
  - Hosted API keys → `slo_tier=hosted`
  - Dedicated API keys (or Dedicated sub-accounts) → `slo_tier=dedicated`
  - Any unrecognized/malformed/legacy principals → `slo_tier=unknown`

### Data model notes
- `deployment_mode` may exist internally (e.g., hosted_shared, dedicated_reserved, private_vpc), but SLO scope for this program is `hosted` and `dedicated`. `private` is out of enforcement scope and will map to `unknown` or a future value once included.

### Telemetry constraints (cardinality)
- Gateway request metrics will include only low-cardinality dimensions by default.
- Dedicated per-account SLO dashboards require `dedicated_account_id` on a narrow set of metrics (request totals, error totals, latency histograms) and only when `slo_tier=dedicated`.
- Hosted will not include any customer identifiers in metrics; customer-level troubleshooting relies on logs/traces and sampled analytics.

### Validation and rollout
- SLO toolkit validation will require that all in-scope services emit `slo_tier` on the canonical metric series.
- Rollout is phased:
  1. Warn-only: `unknown` tier is tracked and surfaced on dashboards; no enforcement.
  2. Soft gating: missing tier tags block new SLO configs for affected endpoint/region.
  3. Hard requirement: SLO/error budget reporting excludes `unknown` traffic from tier-specific dashboards and flags as a data-quality incident when above threshold.

### Data-quality SLO for tagging
We will track:
- `% requests with slo_tier != unknown` by endpoint_class/region.
Target:
- Dedicated: >= 99.99% correctly tagged
- Hosted: >= 99.9% correctly tagged

## Consequences
### Positive
- Accurate and explainable tier-specific SLO/error budget accounting.
- Enables Dedicated customer reporting without polluting Hosted metrics.
- Improves incident triage: responders can immediately see if an event is tier-scoped.

### Negative / trade-offs
- Requires careful coordination to ensure tags survive all hops (gateway → routing → serving → telemetry).
- Dedicated per-account metrics introduce bounded cardinality; must be actively managed (allowlist, relabeling, and periodic audits).

## Security / Privacy considerations
- `dedicated_account_id` is an internal identifier and must not expose customer names.
- Any customer-identifying tags must not be emitted on public/shared dashboards.
- Access to Dedicated account dashboards is controlled via RBAC (Console + Grafana).

## Open questions
- Private tier tagging strategy once Private is included in enforcement (future ADR).
- Exact treatment of multi-account keys and sub-tenant rollups for Dedicated customers with multiple environments.

## Action items
- Implement ingress tier tagging and propagation (edge-gw, routing context, OTel trace propagation). (Owner: Amara Diallo / Mina Haddad)
- Update dashboards and recording rules to require `slo_tier`. (Owner: Sergio Costa)
- Add SLO toolkit validation for metric coverage and missing tier tags. (Owner: Logan Wright)
- Document SLI formulas and exclusions with tier semantics, including fallback accounting. (Owner: Aisha Bello)

## Appendix: Controlled vocabularies
- `slo_tier`: `hosted`, `dedicated`, `unknown`
- `execution_tier`: `dedicated_pool`, `hosted_pool`, `fallback_pool`, `unknown`
- `fallback_reason` (initial): `capacity`, `health`, `policy`, `region_failover`

---
Changelog:
- 2026-02-06: Clarified dedicated per-account metric allowlist and introduced `execution_tier` as analysis-only.
- 2026-02-02: Added data-quality targets for `% tagged` coverage and phased rollout notes.
text:
## Status
Published (effective for SLO reporting + error budget enforcement starting Q1 2026 rollout).

## Context / Problem
Redwood is formalizing tiered SLOs and error budgets for **Hosted (shared)** vs **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**. We must be able to compute SLIs (success rate, latency percentiles, streaming reliability) with correct tier attribution across:
- API Gateway handling (auth, rate limits, request normalization)
- Smart Routing (policy evaluation, fallback, retries)
- Model serving request path (batching, execution)
- Observability pipelines (metrics, logs, traces)

We have recurring reliability escalations tied to burst traffic where:
- Requests may be **retried** or **routed to fallback capacity**.
- Failures can occur at multiple layers (gateway, routing, serving, stream transport).
- Dedicated customers need **account-level visibility**, while Hosted must avoid **metric cardinality explosions**.

Prior to this ADR, tier attribution was inconsistent:
- Some metrics inferred tier from where the request was executed (e.g., which GPU pool), which breaks when fallback occurs.
- Some services did not propagate tier context, causing “unknown” attribution and gaps in SLO accounting.

**Requirement:** Tier attribution must reflect the customer’s contracted service tier (Hosted vs Dedicated), not the execution path chosen at runtime, and must be preserved end-to-end so that SLO/error budget calculations are correct and explainable.

## Decision
We will **attach a tier-aware SLI tag set at ingress (API Gateway)** and **propagate it through routing, retries, fallback, and serving** via request context, trace attributes, and metric labels.

Specifically:
1) The **API Gateway (edge-gw)** is the source of truth for tier classification. It sets tier tags immediately after authentication and before request dispatch.
2) The tier tags are treated as **immutable** for the lifetime of the request (including internal retries). Downstream systems may add analysis tags (e.g., where it executed) but **must not overwrite the ingress tier**.
3) SLI metrics used for SLO/error budgets will be computed primarily from gateway + routing boundary metrics, with tier tags included as first-class dimensions.

## Tier tag set (canonical)
### Required tags (present on all in-scope request metrics)
- `slo_tier`: one of `hosted` | `dedicated` | `unknown`
  - Meaning: tier to be used for SLO/error budget accounting.
- `endpoint_class`: one of `chat` | `completions` | `embeddings` | `rerank` | `other`
  - Meaning: stable grouping used for SLO targets and dashboards.
- `region`: standard Redwood region identifier (e.g., `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`).
- `streaming`: `true` | `false`

### Optional tags (allowed, with constraints)
- `dedicated_account_id`: stable internal account identifier (string).
  - **Only emitted for `slo_tier=dedicated`** and only on an allowlisted metric set used by Dedicated SLO dashboards.
  - Must not be emitted for Hosted to avoid cardinality blowups.
- `execution_tier`: `dedicated_pool` | `hosted_pool` | `fallback_pool` | `unknown`
  - Meaning: where the request actually executed. Used for analysis and mitigation; **not used** for SLO accounting.
- `fallback_reason`: small controlled vocabulary (e.g., `capacity`, `health`, `policy`, `region_failover`).
  - Must be rate-limited and taxonomy-controlled.

### Explicit non-goals / forbidden in high-volume metrics
- No per-model, per-customer, per-api-key dimensions on gateway request counters/histograms (except the Dedicated allowlist noted above).
- No raw customer names/slugs in metric labels.

## Propagation rules
### Ingress tagging (edge-gw)
- edge-gw resolves tier using the authenticated principal (API key / token) via Control Plane metadata.
- edge-gw attaches tier tags to:
  - internal request context (headers or metadata, service-to-service)
  - trace context (OTel attributes / baggage)
  - gateway metrics (Prometheus labels / OTLP metric attributes)

### Routing and fallback
- Routing **must preserve `slo_tier`** from the ingress context.
- If routing chooses fallback execution (e.g., Dedicated spilling to a compatible pool), it adds `execution_tier` and `fallback_reason` but does not change `slo_tier`.
- If tier is missing or `unknown`, routing does not attempt to infer tier from pool selection for SLO purposes.

### Retries
- Internal retries (gateway or routing) keep the same `slo_tier`.
- SLI accounting rules for retries are handled in the SLI specification (e.g., whether we count request-level vs attempt-level failures), but tier tags must be consistent across attempts.

### Streaming
- For streaming requests, `slo_tier` must be present on:
  - stream-open events
  - stream-close events
  - disconnect/error events
- Disconnection events must preserve the original tier even if subsequent fallback is attempted (e.g., reconnect behavior).

## Options considered
### Option A (Chosen): Tag at ingress and propagate
Pros:
- Single source of truth for tier attribution.
- Correct under fallback, retries, and multi-hop execution.
- Matches customer expectation: tier is a contract/property of the customer, not of a GPU pool.
Cons:
- Requires coordinated plumbing across gateway, routing, telemetry, and dashboards.

### Option B: Infer tier from execution pool
Pros:
- Simplifies tagging for serving/runtime.
Cons:
- Incorrect when Dedicated falls back to shared capacity.
- Breaks SLO accounting and customer reporting; leads to under/over-counting dedicated impact.

### Option C: Determine tier in routing (not gateway)
Pros:
- Routing already has policy context.
Cons:
- Gateway-level failures (auth proxying, overload shedding, request parsing, upstream 5xx) would be un-attributed.
- Adds complexity during partial outages when routing metadata is unavailable.

## Rationale
Tiered SLOs and error budgets require:
- **Correct attribution** even when the system behaves dynamically (fallback, retries, shifting regions).
- **Consistency** across metrics, traces, and logs.
- **Controlled cardinality** to keep observability cost and reliability manageable.

Ingress (edge-gw) is the only location that:
- Always sees the request for in-scope endpoints.
- Has stable access to identity metadata (API key → account → tier).
- Can tag before any failure modes in routing/serving occur.

## Implementation details
### Source of tier truth
- Control Plane will expose a simple mapping for authenticated principals:
  - `principal_id` → `account_id`, `deployment_mode`, `slo_tier`
- Canonical mapping:
  - Hosted API keys → `slo_tier=hosted`
  - Dedicated API keys (or Dedicated sub-accounts) → `slo_tier=dedicated`
  - Any unrecognized/malformed/legacy principals → `slo_tier=unknown`

### Data model notes
- `deployment_mode` may exist internally (e.g., hosted_shared, dedicated_reserved, private_vpc), but SLO scope for this program is `hosted` and `dedicated`. `private` is out of enforcement scope and will map to `unknown` or a future value once included.

- Dedicated per-account SLO dashboards require `dedicated_account_id` on a narrow set of metrics (request totals, error totals, latency histograms) and only when `slo_tier=dedicated`.
- Hosted will not include any customer identifiers in metrics; customer-level troubleshooting relies on logs/traces and sampled analytics.

### Validation and rollout
- SLO toolkit validation will require that all in-scope services emit `slo_tier` on the canonical metric series.
- Rollout is phased:
  1. Warn-only: `unknown` tier is tracked and surfaced on dashboards; no enforcement.
  2. Soft gating: missing tier tags block new SLO configs for affected endpoint/region.
  3. Hard requirement: SLO/error budget reporting excludes `unknown` traffic from tier-specific dashboards and flags as a data-quality incident when above threshold.

### Data-quality SLO for tagging
We will track:

Target:

- Hosted: >= 99.9% correctly tagged

## Consequences
### Positive
- Accurate and explainable tier-specific SLO/error budget accounting.
- Enables Dedicated customer reporting without polluting Hosted metrics.
- Improves incident triage: responders can immediately see if an event is tier-scoped.

### Negative / trade-offs

- Dedicated per-account metrics introduce bounded cardinality; must be actively managed (allowlist, relabeling, and periodic audits).

## Security / Privacy considerations
- `dedicated_account_id` is an internal identifier and must not expose customer names.
- Any customer-identifying tags must not be emitted on public/shared dashboards.
- Access to Dedicated account dashboards is controlled via RBAC (Console + Grafana).

## Open questions
- Private tier tagging strategy once Private is included in enforcement (future ADR).
- Exact treatment of multi-account keys and sub-tenant rollups for Dedicated customers with multiple environments.

## Action items
- Implement ingress tier tagging and propagation (edge-gw, routing context, OTel trace propagation). (Owner: Amara Diallo / Mina Haddad)
- Update dashboards and recording rules to require `slo_tier`. (Owner: Sergio Costa)
- Add SLO toolkit validation for metric coverage and missing tier tags. (Owner: Logan Wright)
- Document SLI formulas and exclusions with tier semantics, including fallback accounting. (Owner: Aisha Bello)

## Appendix: Controlled vocabularies
- `slo_tier`: `hosted`, `dedicated`, `unknown`
- `execution_tier`: `dedicated_pool`, `hosted_pool`, `fallback_pool`, `unknown`
- `fallback_reason` (initial): `capacity`, `health`, `policy`, `region_failover`

---

- 2026-02-06: Clarified dedicated per-account metric allowlist and introduced `execution_tier` as analysis-only.
- 2026-02-02: Added data-quality targets for `% tagged` coverage and phased rollout notes.

source.title: ADR-0231: Require quality evaluation gates for Optimize config changes
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
## Status
**Accepted** (effective for Hosted API Optimize applies starting limited production rollout).

## Decision
Redwood Inference will require **quality evaluation gates** to pass **before** any Optimize recommendation (or manual Optimize-driven change) can be applied to **production traffic** for the following config change types:

- **Batching policy updates** (e.g., max batch size, continuous batching parameters)
- **Prefix/KV caching settings** (e.g., cache enablement, eviction policies, cache keying changes)
- **Quantization profile changes** (e.g., fp16 -> int8, model-family-specific quant profiles per GPU SKU)

Gate enforcement is **mandatory** for:
- One-click “Apply” in Console
- Any Optimize auto-apply mechanism
- Canary promotion in rollout controller (defense-in-depth)

A change may only proceed when:
1) **Static validation** passes (unsupported combinations rejected pre-eval)
2) Required **eval gate tier(s)** pass (smoke and/or full)
3) For canary-based rollouts, **online canary signals** remain within allowed bounds during the canary window

**Overrides** are allowed but must be:
- **Time-bound** (expiration required)
- **Role-restricted** (approved by designated approvers)
- **Audited** (reason + artifacts + owner + follow-up recorded)

## Context / Problem
Optimize can recommend cost-saving configuration changes that materially affect runtime execution. While many of these changes are intended to be “semantics-preserving,” in practice they can change:

- Numerical behavior (especially quantization and kernel selection edge cases)
- Long-context behavior and KV cache correctness
- Output quality under specific prompt distributions (e.g., tool-use, multi-turn chat, long-form generation)
- Tail latency in ways that alter downstream product behavior (timeouts, truncation)

Historically, Redwood relied on a combination of:
- Engineer review for high-risk changes
- Monitoring and incident response
- Ad-hoc offline evals (non-standardized)

As Optimize adoption increases, this approach does not scale and creates unacceptable risk of **silent quality regressions** that are only noticed after broad rollout.

## Drivers / Goals
This ADR is motivated by:

- **Prevent regressions by construction:** Block known-bad changes before any user impact.
- **Standardize “quality” for serving changes:** Shared, measurable pass/fail criteria across teams.
- **Traceability:** Provide an audit trail that answers “what changed, why, and who approved it?”
- **Enterprise readiness:** Many customers require change controls, evidence, and the ability to export audit logs.
- **Fast developer experience:** Tiered gates (smoke vs full) so most changes get quick feedback.

Non-goals (for this ADR):
- Full automation for Private/on-prem environments in the initial release (manual workflow is acceptable initially).

## Decision Details
### Scope of enforcement
**In-scope config changes (MVP / v1):**
- Batching policy knobs used by the serving runtime scheduler
- Prefix/KV caching knobs that affect caching behavior or keying
- Quantization profile selection per model family and hardware SKU

**Out of scope (initially):**
- Prompt/content changes (handled by separate prompt governance)
- Model upgrades / new model onboarding (integrate with existing eval process; not redesigned here)

### What “gated” means
A proposed config diff must be associated with:
- A **baseline** (current production config for the same route/model/hardware policy)
- A **gate run** (smoke and/or full eval suites) that produces:
  - Metric deltas vs baseline
  - Statistical confidence / variance estimates
  - Artifacts (per-prompt outputs, rubric traces, logs)

The Optimize service must treat a gate run as a first-class object with a stable identifier and lifecycle:
- `PENDING` → `RUNNING` → `PASSED` | `FAILED` | `CANCELED` | `OVERRIDDEN`

### Required gate tiers
Gate tiers are chosen to bound time and cost:

- **Smoke gate (fast):** Small, high-signal prompt subset; intended to complete quickly to unblock iteration.
- **Full gate:** Broader prompt-set coverage + stricter statistical requirements; required for high-risk diffs and for production rollout expansion.

Policy (initial):
- Batching/caching changes: smoke gate required; full gate required when the diff crosses a risk threshold (see “Risk-based policy”).
- Quantization profile changes: full gate required by default due to higher observed quality variance.

### Risk-based policy
We will maintain a policy mapping config diffs to required gate tier(s) based on:
- Change type (batching vs caching vs quant)
- Model family
- Hardware SKU
- Known “unsafe” combinations from runtime safety catalog

This policy is configurable per route/account tier (e.g., enterprise routes may require full gates more often).

### Enforcement points (defense in depth)
Enforcement must happen in more than one place:

1) **Optimize Apply boundary (primary):**
   - Any attempt to apply a config diff must reference a passing gate run (or valid override).
   - Prevents both UI-based and API-based bypass.

2) **Rollout controller promotion (secondary):**
   - Canary promotion (e.g., 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%) is blocked if the gate is not passing.
   - Adds resilience if other systems misconfigure or fail open.

3) **Audit logging (mandatory):**
   - Emit audit events for: gate started/completed, pass/fail, apply attempted, apply blocked, override requested/granted/expired.

## Alternatives Considered
### A1) Monitor-only (no pre-deploy gates)
**Summary:** Apply Optimize changes and rely on dashboards/alerts to detect regressions.

**Pros:**
- Fastest path for cost savings
- Minimal infrastructure and process

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Detects regressions only after user impact
- Quality signals are often delayed, noisy, or absent for some workloads
- Hard to attribute failures to a specific config diff (confounded by traffic mix, model drift)
- Does not meet enterprise expectations for pre-change evidence

### A2) Post-deploy rollback only
**Summary:** Allow deploy, then rollback if online canary metrics regress.

**Pros:**
- Can catch issues that offline evals miss
- Keeps initial iteration speed

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Still ships regressions to real users during the detection window
- Rollback can be slow or incomplete in the presence of multi-region propagation and caching effects
- Online-only signals are insufficient for some regressions (e.g., subtle rubric quality changes)

### A3) Opt-in gating (per-team or per-route)
**Summary:** Provide gates as tooling but let teams choose.

**Pros:**
- Fewer initial workflow disruptions
- Teams can adopt at their own pace

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Incentives favor skipping gates when under delivery pressure
- Leads to inconsistent quality standards and a confusing customer story
- Fails to prevent “silent regressions” on long-tail routes

### A4) Manual approvals only (no standardized eval requirement)
**Summary:** Require an approver but not a passing eval suite.

**Pros:**
- Clear accountability
- Low engineering effort

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Humans cannot reliably predict quality impact from low-level runtime diffs
- Does not scale and becomes a bottleneck
- Increases variance in decision quality and creates uneven standards

### A5) Online-only gates (canary SLOs as the gate)
**Summary:** Replace offline evals with canary quality signals.

**Pros:**
- Measures real traffic behavior
- Avoids offline prompt-set maintenance

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Some quality attributes do not have reliable online proxies
- Can take too long to reach statistical power
- Can be confounded by routing/caching and traffic shifts

## Consequences
### Positive
- Significant reduction in probability of silent quality regressions from cost optimizations.
- Standardizes what “safe to apply” means for runtime-affecting config changes.
- Provides an audit trail suitable for enterprise security reviews.
- Creates shared infrastructure that can later extend to Dedicated and Private workflows.

### Negative / Costs
- Additional latency before changes can be applied (especially for full gates).
- GPU/compute spend for running eval suites.
- Ongoing maintenance burden for prompt sets and flake management.
- Risk of false positives (flaky suites) blocking legitimate cost savings.

### Mitigations
- Tiered gates (smoke first) to reduce iteration time.
- Rerun policies + flake scoring, with quarantine workflows for flaky prompt sets.
- Clear override policy with post-override follow-ups.
- Bounded budgets and SLAs for gate execution; capacity planning for eval infrastructure.

## Security, Compliance, and Audit
- All gate runs must be attributable to: route/model, diff hash, baseline reference, and requesting identity.
- Artifacts must be stored with retention aligned to audit requirements (policy defined in observability/retention standards).
- Override events are security-sensitive and must be exported through audit log pipelines (SIEM-friendly where applicable).

## Implementation Notes (Non-normative)
This ADR does not prescribe the full architecture, but the accepted approach assumes:

- Optimize service owns the gate-run state machine and persistence.
- Eval-harness provides a trigger API accepting (config diff + baseline) and returning structured results and artifact pointers.
- Console surfaces gate status, failure reasons, and artifact links; blocks Apply when gate is failing.
- Rollout controller consults gate status prior to canary promotion.

## Rollout Plan (High level)
1) Internal beta: enforce gates in staging + select internal routes; stabilize flake/rerun policies.
2) Limited production: enforce for a subset of hosted routes/accounts; require overrides for any bypass.
3) GA: make gates default for Optimize applies in hosted API; expand policy coverage; begin Dedicated defaults.

## Open Questions
- Final thresholds by model family for quantization deltas (tracked in metrics/thresholds spec).
- Default gate tier policy by customer tier (self-serve vs enterprise).
- Artifact retention durations by customer segment and whether customer-specific prompt artifacts require additional redaction.

## Owner
Engineering owner: **Priya Natarajan** (Platform). Product owner: **Aditya Rao** (Optimize).

## Revision History
- 2025-11-18: Initial ADR drafted and circulated for review.
- 2025-12-02: Updated to include defense-in-depth enforcement at rollout controller.
- 2026-02-05: Updated rollout wording after limited production readiness review; clarified override constraints.

**Pros:**
- Can catch issues that offline evals miss
- Keeps initial iteration speed

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Still ships regressions to real users during the detection window
- Rollback can be slow or incomplete in the presence of multi-region propagation and caching effects
- Online-only signals are insufficient for some regressions (e.g., subtle rubric quality changes)

### A3) Opt-in gating (per-team or per-route)
**Summary:** Provide gates as tooling but let teams choose.

**Pros:**
- Fewer initial workflow disruptions
- Teams can adopt at their own pace

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Incentives favor skipping gates when under delivery pressure
- Leads to inconsistent quality standards and a confusing customer story
- Fails to prevent “silent regressions” on long-tail routes

### A4) Manual approvals only (no standardized eval requirement)
**Summary:** Require an approver but not a passing eval suite.

**Pros:**
- Clear accountability
- Low engineering effort

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Humans cannot reliably predict quality impact from low-level runtime diffs
- Does not scale and becomes a bottleneck
- Increases variance in decision quality and creates uneven standards

### A5) Online-only gates (canary SLOs as the gate)
**Summary:** Replace offline evals with canary quality signals.

**Pros:**
- Measures real traffic behavior
- Avoids offline prompt-set maintenance

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Some quality attributes do not have reliable online proxies
- Can take too long to reach statistical power
- Can be confounded by routing/caching and traffic shifts

## Consequences
### Positive
- Significant reduction in probability of silent quality regressions from cost optimizations.
- Standardizes what “safe to apply” means for runtime-affecting config changes.
- Provides an audit trail suitable for enterprise security reviews.
- Creates shared infrastructure that can later extend to Dedicated and Private workflows.

### Negative / Costs
- Additional latency before changes can be applied (especially for full gates).
- GPU/compute spend for running eval suites.
- Ongoing maintenance burden for prompt sets and flake management.
- Risk of false positives (flaky suites) blocking legitimate cost savings.

### Mitigations
- Tiered gates (smoke first) to reduce iteration time.
- Rerun policies + flake scoring, with quarantine workflows for flaky prompt sets.
- Clear override policy with post-override follow-ups.
- Bounded budgets and SLAs for gate execution; capacity planning for eval infrastructure.

## Security, Compliance, and Audit
- All gate runs must be attributable to: route/model, diff hash, baseline reference, and requesting identity.
- Artifacts must be stored with retention aligned to audit requirements (policy defined in observability/retention standards).
- Override events are security-sensitive and must be exported through audit log pipelines (SIEM-friendly where applicable).

## Implementation Notes (Non-normative)
This ADR does not prescribe the full architecture, but the accepted approach assumes:

- Optimize service owns the gate-run state machine and persistence.
- Eval-harness provides a trigger API accepting (config diff + baseline) and returning structured results and artifact pointers.
- Console surfaces gate status, failure reasons, and artifact links; blocks Apply when gate is failing.
- Rollout controller consults gate status prior to canary promotion.

## Rollout Plan (High level)
1) Internal beta: enforce gates in staging + select internal routes; stabilize flake/rerun policies.
2) Limited production: enforce for a subset of hosted routes/accounts; require overrides for any bypass.
3) GA: make gates default for Optimize applies in hosted API; expand policy coverage; begin Dedicated defaults.

## Open Questions
- Final thresholds by model family for quantization deltas (tracked in metrics/thresholds spec).
- Default gate tier policy by customer tier (self-serve vs enterprise).
- Artifact retention durations by customer segment and whether customer-specific prompt artifacts require additional redaction.

## Owner
Engineering owner: **Priya Natarajan** (Platform). Product owner: **Aditya Rao** (Optimize).

## Revision History
- 2025-11-18: Initial ADR drafted and circulated for review.
- 2025-12-02: Updated to include defense-in-depth enforcement at rollout controller.
- 2026-02-05: Updated rollout wording after limited production readiness review; clarified override constraints.
text:
## Status
**Accepted** (effective for Hosted API Optimize applies starting limited production rollout).

## Decision
Redwood Inference will require **quality evaluation gates** to pass **before** any Optimize recommendation (or manual Optimize-driven change) can be applied to **production traffic** for the following config change types:

- **Batching policy updates** (e.g., max batch size, continuous batching parameters)
- **Prefix/KV caching settings** (e.g., cache enablement, eviction policies, cache keying changes)
- **Quantization profile changes** (e.g., fp16 -> int8, model-family-specific quant profiles per GPU SKU)

Gate enforcement is **mandatory** for:
- One-click “Apply” in Console
- Any Optimize auto-apply mechanism
- Canary promotion in rollout controller (defense-in-depth)

A change may only proceed when:
1) **Static validation** passes (unsupported combinations rejected pre-eval)
2) Required **eval gate tier(s)** pass (smoke and/or full)
3) For canary-based rollouts, **online canary signals** remain within allowed bounds during the canary window

**Overrides** are allowed but must be:
- **Time-bound** (expiration required)
- **Role-restricted** (approved by designated approvers)
- **Audited** (reason + artifacts + owner + follow-up recorded)

## Context / Problem
Optimize can recommend cost-saving configuration changes that materially affect runtime execution. While many of these changes are intended to be “semantics-preserving,” in practice they can change:

- Numerical behavior (especially quantization and kernel selection edge cases)
- Long-context behavior and KV cache correctness
- Output quality under specific prompt distributions (e.g., tool-use, multi-turn chat, long-form generation)
- Tail latency in ways that alter downstream product behavior (timeouts, truncation)

Historically, Redwood relied on a combination of:
- Engineer review for high-risk changes
- Monitoring and incident response
- Ad-hoc offline evals (non-standardized)

As Optimize adoption increases, this approach does not scale and creates unacceptable risk of **silent quality regressions** that are only noticed after broad rollout.

## Drivers / Goals
This ADR is motivated by:

- **Prevent regressions by construction:** Block known-bad changes before any user impact.
- **Standardize “quality” for serving changes:** Shared, measurable pass/fail criteria across teams.
- **Traceability:** Provide an audit trail that answers “what changed, why, and who approved it?”
- **Enterprise readiness:** Many customers require change controls, evidence, and the ability to export audit logs.
- **Fast developer experience:** Tiered gates (smoke vs full) so most changes get quick feedback.

Non-goals (for this ADR):
- Full automation for Private/on-prem environments in the initial release (manual workflow is acceptable initially).

## Decision Details
### Scope of enforcement
**In-scope config changes (MVP / v1):**
- Batching policy knobs used by the serving runtime scheduler
- Prefix/KV caching knobs that affect caching behavior or keying
- Quantization profile selection per model family and hardware SKU

**Out of scope (initially):**
- Prompt/content changes (handled by separate prompt governance)
- Model upgrades / new model onboarding (integrate with existing eval process; not redesigned here)

### What “gated” means
A proposed config diff must be associated with:
- A **baseline** (current production config for the same route/model/hardware policy)
- A **gate run** (smoke and/or full eval suites) that produces:
  - Metric deltas vs baseline
  - Statistical confidence / variance estimates
  - Artifacts (per-prompt outputs, rubric traces, logs)

The Optimize service must treat a gate run as a first-class object with a stable identifier and lifecycle:
- `PENDING` → `RUNNING` → `PASSED` | `FAILED` | `CANCELED` | `OVERRIDDEN`

### Required gate tiers
Gate tiers are chosen to bound time and cost:

- **Smoke gate (fast):** Small, high-signal prompt subset; intended to complete quickly to unblock iteration.
- **Full gate:** Broader prompt-set coverage + stricter statistical requirements; required for high-risk diffs and for production rollout expansion.

Policy (initial):
- Batching/caching changes: smoke gate required; full gate required when the diff crosses a risk threshold (see “Risk-based policy”).
- Quantization profile changes: full gate required by default due to higher observed quality variance.

### Risk-based policy
We will maintain a policy mapping config diffs to required gate tier(s) based on:
- Change type (batching vs caching vs quant)
- Model family
- Hardware SKU
- Known “unsafe” combinations from runtime safety catalog

This policy is configurable per route/account tier (e.g., enterprise routes may require full gates more often).

### Enforcement points (defense in depth)
Enforcement must happen in more than one place:

1) **Optimize Apply boundary (primary):**
   - Any attempt to apply a config diff must reference a passing gate run (or valid override).
   - Prevents both UI-based and API-based bypass.

2) **Rollout controller promotion (secondary):**
   - Canary promotion (e.g., 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%) is blocked if the gate is not passing.
   - Adds resilience if other systems misconfigure or fail open.

3) **Audit logging (mandatory):**
   - Emit audit events for: gate started/completed, pass/fail, apply attempted, apply blocked, override requested/granted/expired.

## Alternatives Considered
### A1) Monitor-only (no pre-deploy gates)
**Summary:** Apply Optimize changes and rely on dashboards/alerts to detect regressions.

**Pros:**
- Fastest path for cost savings
- Minimal infrastructure and process

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Detects regressions only after user impact
- Quality signals are often delayed, noisy, or absent for some workloads
- Hard to attribute failures to a specific config diff (confounded by traffic mix, model drift)
- Does not meet enterprise expectations for pre-change evidence

### A2) Post-deploy rollback only
**Summary:** Allow deploy, then rollback if online canary metrics regress.

**Pros:**
- Can catch issues that offline evals miss
- Keeps initial iteration speed

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Still ships regressions to real users during the detection window
- Rollback can be slow or incomplete in the presence of multi-region propagation and caching effects
- Online-only signals are insufficient for some regressions (e.g., subtle rubric quality changes)

### A3) Opt-in gating (per-team or per-route)
**Summary:** Provide gates as tooling but let teams choose.

**Pros:**
- Fewer initial workflow disruptions
- Teams can adopt at their own pace

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Incentives favor skipping gates when under delivery pressure
- Leads to inconsistent quality standards and a confusing customer story
- Fails to prevent “silent regressions” on long-tail routes

### A4) Manual approvals only (no standardized eval requirement)
**Summary:** Require an approver but not a passing eval suite.

**Pros:**
- Clear accountability
- Low engineering effort

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Humans cannot reliably predict quality impact from low-level runtime diffs
- Does not scale and becomes a bottleneck
- Increases variance in decision quality and creates uneven standards

### A5) Online-only gates (canary SLOs as the gate)
**Summary:** Replace offline evals with canary quality signals.

**Pros:**
- Measures real traffic behavior
- Avoids offline prompt-set maintenance

**Cons / Why rejected:**
- Some quality attributes do not have reliable online proxies
- Can take too long to reach statistical power
- Can be confounded by routing/caching and traffic shifts

## Consequences
### Positive
- Significant reduction in probability of silent quality regressions from cost optimizations.
- Standardizes what “safe to apply” means for runtime-affecting config changes.
- Provides an audit trail suitable for enterprise security reviews.
- Creates shared infrastructure that can later extend to Dedicated and Private workflows.

### Negative / Costs
- Additional latency before changes can be applied (especially for full gates).
- GPU/compute spend for running eval suites.
- Ongoing maintenance burden for prompt sets and flake management.
- Risk of false positives (flaky suites) blocking legitimate cost savings.

### Mitigations
- Tiered gates (smoke first) to reduce iteration time.
- Rerun policies + flake scoring, with quarantine workflows for flaky prompt sets.
- Clear override policy with post-override follow-ups.
- Bounded budgets and SLAs for gate execution; capacity planning for eval infrastructure.

## Security, Compliance, and Audit
- All gate runs must be attributable to: route/model, diff hash, baseline reference, and requesting identity.
- Artifacts must be stored with retention aligned to audit requirements (policy defined in observability/retention standards).
- Override events are security-sensitive and must be exported through audit log pipelines (SIEM-friendly where applicable).

## Implementation Notes (Non-normative)
This ADR does not prescribe the full architecture, but the accepted approach assumes:

- Optimize service owns the gate-run state machine and persistence.
- Eval-harness provides a trigger API accepting (config diff + baseline) and returning structured results and artifact pointers.
- Console surfaces gate status, failure reasons, and artifact links; blocks Apply when gate is failing.
- Rollout controller consults gate status prior to canary promotion.

## Rollout Plan (High level)
1) Internal beta: enforce gates in staging + select internal routes; stabilize flake/rerun policies.
2) Limited production: enforce for a subset of hosted routes/accounts; require overrides for any bypass.
3) GA: make gates default for Optimize applies in hosted API; expand policy coverage; begin Dedicated defaults.

## Open Questions
- Final thresholds by model family for quantization deltas (tracked in metrics/thresholds spec).
- Default gate tier policy by customer tier (self-serve vs enterprise).
- Artifact retention durations by customer segment and whether customer-specific prompt artifacts require additional redaction.

## Owner
